issues.title: Mechanism to opt-in or opt-out of markdown formatting
issues.body:
Once we have more of the feature set completed and want to stabilize support, there are a few paths we could take:

- Require users who want markdown formatting on-by-default to set `extend-include = ["**/*.md"]` in their project config (opt-in).
- Add a new `format.markdown-code-blocks = true` config option, similar to docstring formatting, that would automatically include `.md` files in the global default search path (opt-in). What do do if the user explicitly passes a `.md` file while the feature is disabled is tbd.
- Turn it on by default, including `.md` in the global default search path, and allow users to set `exclude = ["**/*.md"]` or similar in their project config if they don't want the feature (opt-out).
issues.author: amyreese

comments.body:
Thinking about this some more (as I was adding a new option in #22750) should the "opt-in" for now simply be something like recommending `extend-include = ["**/*.md"]` and let `ruff format foo.md` just work even without needing `--preview` or a dedicated config option?

@MichaReiser @ntBre

Ah sorry. I didn't see your comment here before posting on the PR.

I don't know. But using `include`/`exclude` is definitely the first thing that comes to mind when it's about defining on which files Ruff should run. I don't have the answer here but we should explore the different options and then decide on a design

I also don't know, but it makes some sense to me to reuse `include` and `exclude`. It does seem a bit annoying to have to `extend-include = ["**/*.md"]` to enable the feature though. Are we planning to make markdown formatting off by default?

The main question to me was whether we add `*.md` to the global/default `INCLUDE` or `INCLUDE_PREVIEW` and use a feature flag similar to `docstring-code-format` to turn on/off actually *formatting* (or linting?) those files, or if we just support the ability to format/lint markdown files with no feature flags, and just leave it to users to add `*.md` to their `include`/`extend-include` lists themselves in order to "turn it on".
Will ask in the main issue if users want ruff to format markdown files by default or want explicit opt-in

Since opinions were requested: I would enable this feature. Formatting code blocks is really neat and I believe something the `dprint` formatter Markdown plugin also does by offloading to its other plugins (similarly with the html/css/ts plugins).

Since I'm using shared configs I don't really care if it's opt-in/out by default. But I'd lean to say opt in until stable/confident enough, then switch in a breaking version.

> Turn it on by default, including `.md` in the global default search path, and allow users to set `exclude = ["**/*.md"]` or similar in their project config if they don't want the feature (opt-out).

i think this is the better choice. i prefer when linters enable all checks by default because it's much better for discoverability. you can be confident that all users who disabled it have done so because they don't want it, and that all users who keep it enabled do.

if this feature was disabled by default, a vast majority of people who don't check changelogs would be missing out on this feature that they would enable if they knew it existed.

I would definitely be using this feature when available. My feeling is it is probably better to be opt-out. I would happily just let ruff do its thing. I can see some people who have opinions to let them be won over by automatic linting. The only exception I can see is where your quite short example spans many lines in markdown due to many arguments in a function (for example). But that could be "fixed" by adjusting the characters per row.
@amyreese what's left to do here?
Oops, I thought I had set one of my PRs to close this. I think this is done, now that `.md` files are discovered by default in preview mode.

issues.title: 0.14.14 breaks block suppresion
issues.body:
### Summary

Here's a comparison between version 0.14.13 and 0.14.14, no code change:

```
$ uv run ruff version
ruff 0.14.13 (b4b8299d6 2026-01-15)

$ rg -B 1 'preview = true' pyproject.toml
82-[tool.ruff.lint]
83:preview = true

$ uv run ruff check
All checks passed!

$ # edit pyproject.toml

$ uv sync
Resolved 191 packages in 131ms
      Built app @ file:///Users/aquarhead/work/a
Prepared 1 package in 19ms
Uninstalled 2 packages in 3ms
Installed 2 packages in 2ms
 ~ app==1.10.0 (from file:///Users/aquarhead/work/a)
 - ruff==0.14.13
 + ruff==0.14.14
 
$ rg -B 1 'preview = true' pyproject.toml
82-[tool.ruff.lint]
83:preview = true

$ uv run ruff version
ruff 0.14.14 (8b2e7b36f 2026-01-22)

$ uv run ruff check
E402 Module level import not at top of file
   --> app/router.py:113:1
    |
112 | # ruff: disable[E402, F401]
113 | import app.demand.worksheets
    | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
114 | import app.supply.worksheets
115 | import app.masterdata.worksheets
    |
... (every line in the block gets 2 errors)
```

Our actual code usage is like this - we have 2 blocks with the exact same suppresion config, not sure if that's relevant:

```py
# ruff: disable[E402, F401]
import app.demand.worksheets
import ...
# ruff: enable[E402, F401]

router.include_router(prefix="/blah", router=dynamic_router) # this relies on the imports above

# ruff: disable[E402, F401]
import ...

# ruff: enable[E402, F401]
```

Not sure if it's the block suppresion or the preview mode check, I think this is the only feature we use from the preview mode.

Here's all the ruff configuration in pyproject.toml:

```toml
[tool.ruff]
line-length = 120
extend-exclude = ["alembic/versions/*.py"]

[tool.ruff.lint]
preview = true
select = [
  "E",   # pycodestyle errors
  "W",   # pycodestyle warnings
  "F",   # pyflakes
  "C",   # flake8-comprehensions
  "B",   # flake8-bugbear
  "DTZ", # flake8-datetimez
  "UP",  # pyupgrade
  "I",   # isort
]
ignore = [
  "E266", # We want more obvious block comments

issues.title: Better extension mapping for markdown files
issues.body: Rather than having an exhaustive list of all possible markdown file extensions in `SourceType::from(<path>)`, maybe this could be configurable in some way? Maybe this could also affect default file discovery when we stabilize markdown formatting?
issues.author: amyreese

comments.body: I think this is what the `--extension` CLI flag is for, right? But it seems only to be exposed in the CLI, not in the configuration, which is surprising to me. There was some discussion of whether or not to make it a config option in https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/pull/8373.

> I think this is what the `--extension` CLI flag is for, right?

That's my preferred path, but it's currently limited to `PySourceType` and depends on other "language" enums that aren't markdown-aware, so there would need to be some refactoring somewhere to make that work. And some way of exposing it in configuration.

Similar in other tools:
- dprint's `associations`: https://dprint.dev/config/#associations
- VSCode's `files.associations`: https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/identifiers
- prettier's `overrides.options.parser`: https://prettier.io/docs/configuration#setting-the-parser-option

issues.title: Resolve overlap between RUF100 and RUF102
issues.body:
When both rules are selected, any `#noqa` with unknown/invalid rule codes will be flagged by both RUF100 and RUF102. The RUF100 diagnostic will treat the code as "unknown", while RUF102 will call it "invalid".

We could either:
- drop the "unknown" codes section from RUF100 entirely and leave that for RUF102
- hide the "unknown" codes section of RUF100 iff the RUF102 rule is enabled

I'm in favor of the first, as it simplifies the code and is easier to "explain" IMO.
issues.author: amyreese

issues.title: Make RUF102 also flag file-level noqa codes
issues.body:
> It turns out that there are actually two instances of this warning: one for file-level `noqa` comments and one for line-level comments. RUF102 only covers the line-level case:
> 
> ```console
> ❯ ruff check --select RUF102 - <<EOF
> # ruff: noqa: X111
> 
> 1 # noqa: X111
> EOF
> warning: Invalid rule code provided to `# ruff: noqa` at -:1: X111
> warning: Invalid rule code provided to `# noqa` at -:3: X111
> RUF102 [*] Invalid rule code in `# noqa`: X111
>  --> -:3:3
>   |
> 1 | # ruff: noqa: X111
> 2 |
> 3 | 1 # noqa: X111
>   |   ^^^^^^^^^^^^
>   |
> help: Remove the `# noqa` comment
> ```
> 
> I'll go ahead and remove the warning for the covered case, but I think we should also make RUF102 cover file-level suppression comments and then remove that warning as well. We'll probably need to make that a preview change since the rule has already been stabilized. 

 _Originally posted by @ntBre in [#23267](https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/23267#issuecomment-3898448613)_
issues.author: amyreese

issues.title: `--add-noqa` for diagnostics on blank lines results in bad whitespace
issues.body:
### Summary

Rules like `D100` can produce diagnostics on `TextRange::ZERO` even if the line is blank.  In these cases, running ruff with `--add-noqa` produces a line with leading whitespace:

Create an empty file, and run `ruff check --add-noqa`:

```
$ echo "" > test.py

$ ruff check --isolated --select D100 --add-noqa test.py
Added 1 noqa directive.

$ cat test.py
  # noqa: D100
```

### Version

main

issues.title: Fix for W391 does not Work for Notebooks
issues.body:
### Summary

Notebooks containing multiple empty cells in a row trigger the warning W391 which is reported as fixable.
When running `ruff --isolated check --select W391 --preview --fix Two_empty_cells.ipynb` ruff will report the error W391 "Extra newline at end of cell" and reports it as fixed. However the cell is not removed, running the command multiple times results in ruff reporting the error every time and each time saying 0 remaining errors:
```bash
$ ruff --isolated check --select W391 --preview --fix Two_empty_cells.ipynb 
Found 1 error (1 fixed, 0 remaining).

$ ruff --isolated check --select W391 --preview --fix Two_empty_cells.ipynb 
Found 1 error (1 fixed, 0 remaining).

$ ruff --isolated check --select W391 --preview --fix Two_empty_cells.ipynb
Found 1 error (1 fixed, 0 remaining).
```

If there are more than two empty cells the `--fix` option causes ruff to panic:
```bash
$ ruff --isolated check --select W391 --preview --fix Three_empty_cells.ipynb                                                                                                            (base) 
panic: Panicked at crates/ruff_notebook/src/notebook.rs:281:21 when checking `Three_empty_cells.ipynb`: `Transformed content out of bounds (25..24) for cell at 2`
--> Three_empty_cells.ipynb:1:1
info: This indicates a bug in Ruff.
info: If you could open an issue at https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/new?title=%5Bpanic%5D, we'd be very appreciative!
info: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to show the full backtrace information

Found 1 error.
error: Panic during linting indicates a bug in Ruff. If you could open an issue at:

https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/new?title=%5BLinter%20panic%5D

...with the relevant file contents, the `pyproject.toml` settings, and the stack trace above, we'd be very appreciative!
```

Notebook files:
[Two_empty_cells.ipynb](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24792457/Two_empty_cells.ipynb)
[Three_empty_cells.ipynb](https://github.c
issues.author: m472

comments.body: Thanks for the report! This reminded me of https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/20789, and in turn https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/14445, which I think report the same panic. The fix not working is a new one to me, though.

issues.title: [Fix error] Syntax Error Bug encountered with pyupgrade and multi-line Union
issues.body:
Just encountered the request for bug report when running ruff:

```
error: Fix introduced a syntax error. Reverting all changes.

This indicates a bug in Ruff. If you could open an issue at:
```

I was able to narrow it down to a pretty small reproducer.  

```sh
$ tree .
.
├── main.py
└── pyproject.toml
```


```toml
# pyproject.toml
[project]
name = "ruffbug"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.10"

[tool.ruff]
line-length = 88
target-version = "py310"
fix = true

[tool.ruff.lint]
select = ["UP"]
```

```python
# main.py
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from typing import Literal, TypeAlias, Union

    LongLiterals: TypeAlias = Union[
        Literal["LongLiteralNumberOne"]
        | Literal["LongLiteralNumberTwo"]
        | Literal["LongLiteralNumberThree"]
    ]
```


```sh
$ uv run ruff check main.py --fix

error: Fix introduced a syntax error. Reverting all changes.

This indicates a bug in Ruff. If you could open an issue at:

    https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/new?title=%5BFix%20error%5D

...quoting the contents of `main.py`, the rule codes UP007, along with the `pyproject.toml` settings and executed command, we'd be very appreciative!

UP007 Use `X | Y` for type annotations
  --> main.py:8:31
   |
 6 |       from typing import Literal, TypeAlias, Union
 7 |
 8 |       LongLiterals: TypeAlias = Union[
   |  _______________________________^
 9 | |         Literal["LongLiteralNumberOne"]
10 | |         | Literal["LongLiteralNumberTwo"]
11 | |         | Literal["LongLiteralNumberThree"]
12 | |     ]
   | |_____^
   |
help: Convert to `X | Y`

Found 1 error.
[*] 1 fixable with the `--fix` option.
```

**importantly**:  If I get rid of that third `Literal["LongLiteralNumberThree"]` (such that formatting would cause the union to fit on a single line), then a round of `ruff format` and `ruff check` work fine.  It seems to only have difficulty with the multi-line union
issues.author: tlambert03

issues.title: UP045 fixes `None | Optional[None | int]` to `None | None | int | None`
issues.body:
### Summary

The underlying issue behind #18508 still exists. [The following example](https://play.ruff.rs/f2035b9d-f908-46cb-bd7d-121e1ab9f86d) is contrived, but it implies that the implementation of [`non-pep604-annotation-optional` (UP045)](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/non-pep604-annotation-optional/) doesn’t properly model the rules of `None` and `|`, instead special-casing specific patterns.

```console
$ cat >up045.py <<'# EOF'
from typing import Optional
foo: None | Optional[None | int] = None
# EOF

$ ruff --isolated check --select UP045 up045.py --target-version py313 --fix
Found 1 error (1 fixed, 0 remaining).

$ cat up045.py
from typing import Optional
foo: None | None | int | None = None

$ python3.13 up045.py 2>&1 | tail -n 1
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for |: 'NoneType' and 'NoneType'
```

### Version

ruff 0.15.1 (a2f11d239 2026-02-12)
issues.author: dscorbett

comments.body: Ah okay, thanks. I guess it wasn't as fixed as I thought.

issues.title: [T203] False positives for non-debugging use
issues.body:
### Summary

All uses of `pprint` are currently flagged as the [documentation notes][docs].

As a heuristic, it would be convenient for me at least if the rule were disapplied if a `stream=` were passed to the call. Needing to direct the output somewhere specific makes it much less likely this is debugging, and automatically suppressing the rule in that case would avoid false-positive noise.

[docs]: https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/p-print/
issues.author: alynn-coefficient

comments.body:
This makes sense to me. We actually have a similar exception for `print` already:

https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/blob/7cd738a0295a00a85af18a46483f45263ad98ecf/crates/ruff_linter/src/rules/flake8_print/rules/print_call.rs#L130-L133

issues.title: RUF036 fix is unsafe and introduces syntax errors
issues.body:
### Summary

The fix for [`none-not-at-end-of-union` (RUF036)](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/none-not-at-end-of-union/) should in general be unsafe because it can change the program’s behavior. [Example](https://play.ruff.rs/a46e5b1b-d741-477d-8dd4-fd18ca6081c4):
```console
$ cat >ruf036_1.py <<'# EOF'
class C:
    def __or__(self, other): return False
    def __ror__(self, other): return True
print(None | C())

from typing import Literal
class M(type):
    def __or__(self, other): return Literal[False]
    def __ror__(self, other): return Literal[True]
class C(metaclass=M): pass
x: None | C
print(__annotate__(1)["x"])
# EOF

$ python3.14 ruf036_1.py
True
typing.Literal[True]

$ ruff --isolated check --select RUF036 --preview ruf036_1.py --fix
Found 2 errors (2 fixed, 0 remaining).

$ python3.14 ruf036_1.py
False
typing.Literal[False]
```

It can still be safe for standard-library types because they behave as expected with `|`. I think it is worth a special case for such types because they are common in annotations and it would be nice for the fix to stay safe when possible.

The fix can introduce a syntax error by concatenating tokens. Spaces should be inserted where appropriate. This doesn’t happen in type expressions, but it happens in general, and it might happen in type expressions someday if anything like [PEP 827](https://peps.python.org/pep-0827/) is accepted. [Example](https://play.ruff.rs/c924a720-80f9-4dad-bbfe-771bdec4cbda):
```console
$ cat >ruf036_2.py <<'# EOF'
print(None | (int)and 2)
print(2 or(None) | int)
# EOF

$ python ruf036_2.py
2
2

$ ruff --isolated check --select RUF036 --preview ruf036_2.py --fix

error: Fix introduced a syntax error. Reverting all changes.

This indicates a bug in Ruff. If you could open an issue at:

    https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/new?title=%5BFix%20error%5D

issues.title: SIM113 only reported once per scope
issues.body:
### Summary

```
i = 0
for val in [1,2,3]:
    print(f"{i}: {val}")
    i += 1

i = 0
for val in [1,2,3]:
    print(f"{i}: {val}")
    i += 1
```

Only the first `i += 1` is reported with SIM113. And if you fix it:

```
for i,val in enumerate([1,2,3]):
    print(f"{i}: {val}")

i = 0
for val in [1,2,3]:
    print(f"{i}: {val}")
    i += 1
```

`i += 1` is still not reported as an error. Now if you change the variable name:

```
for i,val in enumerate([1,2,3]):
    print(f"{i}: {val}")

j = 0
for val in [1,2,3]:
    print(f"{j}: {val}")
    j += 1
```

Now `j += 1` is reported with SIM113.

### Version

_No response_
issues.author: jporcher

issues.title: SLF001 special-cases the name `self`
issues.body:
### Summary

[`private-member-access` (SLF001)](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/private-member-access/) has false positives and false negatives because it keys off the parameter name `self`. The name is just a convention; what really matters is that it is the first parameter of an instance method. If that parameter is not named `self`, there is a false positive. If a different parameter is named `self`, there is a false negative. If a parameter of a non-instance method (see [`classmethod-decorators`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#lint_pep8-naming_classmethod-decorators) and [`staticmethod-decorators`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#lint_pep8-naming_staticmethod-decorators)) or a non-method is named `self`, there is a false negative. If a non-parameter variable is named `self`, there is a false negative. [Example](https://play.ruff.rs/5fcc820f-680a-4825-b4e5-196586898012):
```console
$ cat >slf001.py <<'# EOF'
class C:
    def false_positive(this):
        this._x = 0

    def false_negative_1(this, self):
        self._x = 1

    @classmethod
    def false_negative_2(self):
        return self._x

    @staticmethod
    def false_negative_3(self):
        return self._x

def false_negative_4(self):
    return self._x

def false_negative_5():
    self = C()
    return self._x

self = C()
def false_negative_6():
    return self._x
# EOF

$ ruff --isolated check --select SLF001 slf001.py                        
SLF001 Private member accessed: `_x`
 --> slf001.py:3:9
  |
1 | class C:
2 |     def false_positive(this):
3 |         this._x = 0
  |         ^^^^^^^
4 |
5 |     def false_negative_1(this, self):
  |

Found 1 error.
```

### Version

ruff 0.15.8 (c2a881584 2026-03-26)
issues.author: dscorbett

issues.title: RUF024 fix shadows a variable named `key`
issues.body:
### Summary

The fix for [`mutable-fromkeys-value` (RUF024)](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/mutable-fromkeys-value/) changes the program’s behavior when the second argument to `dict.fromkeys` uses a variable named `key` because the fix creates a new `key` that shadows it. The best solution is for Ruff to use a `gensym`-like function to try e.g. `key` then `key_0`, `key_1`, etc. until it finds an unused name. This would benefit all fixes that create new variables. [Example](https://play.ruff.rs/76f9db21-f943-4010-9f64-393ac9218b30):
```console
$ cat >ruf024.py <<'# EOF'
key = "xy"
print(dict.fromkeys("ABC", list(key)))
# EOF

$ python ruf024.py
{'A': ['x', 'y'], 'B': ['x', 'y'], 'C': ['x', 'y']}

$ ruff --isolated check ruf024.py --select RUF024 --unsafe-fixes --fix
Found 1 error (1 fixed, 0 remaining).

$ cat ruf024.py
key = "xy"
print({key: list(key) for key in "ABC"})

$ python ruf024.py
{'A': ['A'], 'B': ['B'], 'C': ['C']}
```

### Version

ruff 0.15.8 (c2a881584 2026-03-26)
issues.author: dscorbett

comments.body: Hello @MichaReiser  I am interested in this issue, could you please assign it to me?
I agree that a gen name function would be the most ideal, but I think it's also fine to not offer a fix for that specific instance.
I think the simpler fix is to check if `key` is already used in scope, and if so, skip offering the autofix(it still reports the diagnostic, just not does fix).

@RenzoMXD a heads up. We no longer participate in the Gittensor program and we'll close all PRs from accounts associated with them. You're welcome to contribute under an account that is not associated with Gittensor.

issues.title: `PYI034`: false positive for in-place operations with return type annotation
issues.body:
### Summary

## [Playground](https://play.ruff.rs/d79d3fbe-e5c2-472a-abcb-7c79602dcb8a)

```py
class A:
    def __iadd__(self, other: int) -> int:  # `__iadd__` methods in classes like `A` usually return `self` at runtime
        return 1
```

```toml
[lint]
select = [ "PYI034" ]
```

this function usually returns `1` at runtime, not `self`, so the error and the quickfix are not correct

if the intent of the rule is to suggest returning `self` instead of any other type for in-place operators, then the wording should be updated accordingly
issues.author: KotlinIsland

comments.body:
The in-place operations are special-cased, and the only time that explicitly tests whether the return type isn't `self` instead of testing whether it matches the class's name (`A` in this case). 

Strictly enforcing that all in-place methods return `self` feels more opinionated to me and should probably be its own rule, if that's in fact something we want to enforce. The reason why I say it's more strict is that Python's documentation explicitly mentions that returning `Self` is only recommended but not required:

>  These methods should attempt to do the operation in-place (modifying self) and return the result (which could be, but does not have to be, self). ([source](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__iadd__))


If the rule's intention is to enforce returning `Self`, then it should also flag the following, which it is not (note the missing return type annotation):

```py
class A:
    def __iadd__(self, other: int):  
        return 1
```

Because of that, I think we should change the condition, even if it means deviating from upstream. But I'd love to hear @AlexWaygood's opinion, because he added support for in-place operations upstream in https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8-pyi/pull/180
See also [PyCQA/flake8-pyi#539](https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8-pyi/issues/539)

is there any value in reporting:
```py
class A:
    def f(self) -> A:  # use `Self` instead of `A`
        return self
```

issues.title: Custom dunder variables and `RUF067`
issues.body:
Could you please collaborate on how `RUF067` should work with packages such as https://github.com/Erotemic/mkinit, which use dunder variables (e.g., `__submodules__`, `__protected__`) to customize the tool's behavior?

**A.** Is this the official Python convention to strictly forbid all non‑official dunder variables? Is there a strict list of "official" variables?

**B.** Should Ruff allow all custom dunder variables?

**C.** Or should Ruff let its users extend an allowlist of variables manually?

_Originally posted by @simon-liebehenschel in https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/22143#issuecomment-3790077552_
issues.author: ntBre

comments.body:
@simon-liebehenschel I moved this to a separate issue for more visibility. Thanks for trying out this new preview rule and giving us this feedback!

To answer your first question, it's definitely not an "official" convention to enforce this rule at all. This is a very opinionated lint rule that may not be a good fit for all projects. In light of that, a couple of additional options are:
- disable the rule entirely
- ignore the rule for a specific file or files
- suppress the rule locally with `noqa`

That said, I'm not opposed to either option B or C if others would find them useful. Are you finding `RUF067` to be generally helpful except for these dunder variables?

I think that simply allowing all dunder variables is a good choice for the rule, as it balances usefulness with avoiding annoyance. Also, assigning a few dunder variables does not seem to have a performance impact (?), which is the main concern of this rule. 

Regarding the original idea of the rule — yes, the rationale is clear.
Thank you! It sounds reasonable to me to allow assignments to any dunder variable when strict mode is off.

Hi @ntBre — I’m going to try a PR for this unless you’d rather steer it another way.

Idea: when strict mode is off, don’t flag assignments if every target is a normal name like `__foo__ `(same` __…__` idea as elsewhere in Ruff). Still flag things like `foo = __bar__ = …` because foo isn’t a dunder. When strict mode is on, leave behavior as it is now.

That would mean stuff like `__path__ = 5` stops warning in non‑strict mode, which matches what you said about allowing any dunder there — but it’s a change from how the rule behaves today. Is that still what you want?

If anything about that sounds off, tell me and I’ll adjust before I open the PR.

issues.title: except* formatting broken for exception unpacking with tuples
issues.body:
### Summary

**Describe the bug**
When using a tuple of exceptions in an `except` block with the unpacking syntax, Ruff’s formatter produces invalid Python code. The formatting chain progressively corrupts the syntax.

**Steps to reproduce**
1. Start with this code:

```python
import orjson

JSON_DECODE_EXCEPTIONS = (orjson.JSONDecodeError,)

def fibonacci(n):
    """Compute the nth number in the Fibonacci sequence."""
    try:
        return 1
    except (*JSON_DECODE_EXCEPTIONS, ValueError):
        return []
```

2. Apply Ruff formatting. It first produces:


```python
import orjson

JSON_DECODE_EXCEPTIONS = (orjson.JSONDecodeError,)


def fibonacci(n):
    """Compute the nth number in the Fibonacci sequence."""
    try:
        return 1
    except *JSON_DECODE_EXCEPTIONS, ValueError:
        return []
```
3. Applying Ruff again, it produces invalid syntax:


```python
import orjson

JSON_DECODE_EXCEPTIONS = (orjson.JSONDecode_EXCEPTIONS,)


def fibonacci(n):
    """Compute the nth number in the Fibonacci sequence."""
    try:
        return 1
    except* JSON_DECODE_EXCEPTIONS, ValueError:
        return []
```
Playground links

Initial formatting: https://play.ruff.rs/671ad30c-c884-4fa6-ab21-8c8f1a5f494f
Second formatting: https://play.ruff.rs/e1fe4e28-8ecb-4af9-9792-22753c51ddcb

See: https://github.com/home-assistant/core/pull/162356

### Version

ruff 0.15.0
issues.author: Thomas55555

comments.body: Thanks for the report!

issues.title: Ignoring `RUF102` does not work
issues.body:
### Summary

This looks like a fun one.

```bash
❯ cat foo.py
x = '123'  # noqa: O103
```

```
❯ cat ruff.toml
[lint]
select = ["E4", "E5", "E7", "E9", "F", "G", "RUF", "S", "UP"]
ignore = ["RUF102"]

❯ ruff check --fix foo.py 
warning: Invalid rule code provided to `# noqa` at foo.py:1: O103
Found 1 error (1 fixed, 0 remaining).
```

I thought I might have the wrong code so I tried `RUF100` through `RUF104`. Curiously it seems I can only disable this warning by specifying *all* 5 codes or using a more generic ignore like `RUF10`.

```
❯ cat ruff.toml
[lint]
select = ["E4", "E5", "E7", "E9", "F", "G", "RUF", "S", "UP"]
ignore = ["RUF100", "RUF101", "RUF102", "RUF103", "RUF104"]

❯ ruff check --fix foo.py 
warning: Invalid rule code provided to `# noqa` at foo/__init__.py:1: O103
All checks passed!
```

You'll note that I still get a warning on the first run though (but not subsequent runs unless I remove `.ruff_cache`). I'd have expected silencing this error to also silence that warning. I'm guessing that warning comes from a different code path that predates `RUF102` and should possibly be removed (?).

Note that I worked around this by using the [`lint.external` setting](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#lint_external) to whitelist the non-ruff linter codes.

### Version

ruff 0.15.1
issues.author: stephenfin

comments.body:
https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/23191 looks related though the reporter notes:

> The global suppression under `[tool.ruff.lint]` works though.

so I'm not sure what's going on/what I've missed.

As you noted, that warning is separate from `RUF102`. I agree that we should remove it now that `RUF102` is a lint rule, thank you for the report!

But yes, otherwise `lint.external` is the recommended approach for non-Ruff rule codes, rather than suppressing `RUF102`.

It turns out that there are actually two instances of this warning: one for file-level `noqa` comments and one for line-level comments. RUF102 only covers the line-level case:

```console
❯ ruff check --select RUF102 - <<EOF
# ruff: noqa: X111

1 # noqa: X111
EOF
warning: Invalid rule code provided to `# ruff: noqa` at -:1: X111
warning: Invalid rule code provided to `# noqa` at -:3: X111
RUF102 [*] Invalid rule code in `# noqa`: X111
 --> -:3:3
  |
1 | # ruff: noqa: X111
2 |
3 | 1 # noqa: X111
  |   ^^^^^^^^^^^^
  |
help: Remove the `# noqa` comment
```

I'll go ahead and remove the warning for the covered case, but I think we should also make RUF102 cover file-level suppression comments and then remove that warning as well. We'll probably need to make that a preview change since the rule has already been stabilized.

issues.title: Edge case in `FormatSpec::parse`
issues.body:
I noticed while reviewing #21069 that a case like this:

```py
f"{1:\x64}"
```

results in an `Err` from [`FormatSpec::parse`](https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/blob/bf726c80e538c96062f4e4ae8541cc478b1dea1c/crates/ruff_python_literal/src/format.rs#L356) despite being valid on Python 3.11:

```pycon
Python 3.11.13 (main, Sep  2 2025, 14:20:25) [Clang 20.1.4 ] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> f"{1:\x64}"
'1'
```

and also 3.14:

```pycon
Python 3.14.2 (main, Dec  9 2025, 19:03:28) [Clang 21.1.4 ] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> f"{1:\x64}"
'1'
```

(and I assume all other versions as well, #21069 was just about the behavior of backslashes in interpolations in 3.11 vs 3.12+).

This seems like quite a niche issue, and you can see that GitHub's syntax highlighting doesn't like it either, but I wanted to open a follow-up issue regardless.

issues.title: `format` produces sematically incorrect output when lambda body contains implicit string concatenation
issues.body:
### Summary

When a lambda contains a implicitly concatenated string where the string is a format string that takes lambda's param as the `format` arg, if the line is too long, `ruff format` will split the string across multiple lines without parenthesizing the string, turning the string into 2 separate strings, breaking the format string.

```python
# Input
foo = lambda x: 'hello this is a really long string that then get concatenated ' 'with another string that prints {x!r}'.format(x=x)

# Ruff
foo = lambda x: "hello this is a really long string that then get concatenated "
"with another string that prints {x!r}".format(x=x)
                                               # ^ NameError: name 'x' is not defined

# Black
foo = (
    lambda x: "hello this is a really long string that then get concatenated "
    "with another string that prints {x!r}".format(x=x)
)
```

https://play.ruff.rs/160f41fc-c9ae-4422-8024-327f48bc2652

I was able to bisect the bug and found the regression begins at b53460799b592e5276e1d148d8a48469f396032e

Friendly ping @dylwil3 

### Version

ruff 0.15.5+172 (fd9568a70 2026-03-09)
issues.author: jamestrew

comments.body:
Thanks for the report! I think Dylan is off the hook for this one, his commit just stabilized the preview behavior I introduced in https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/pull/22744. The `format` call on the end is triggering the special case for call expressions and subscripts here:

https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/blob/7200fb96bd0b9fd5e73f1b41778cdc33583bd4ae/crates/ruff_python_formatter/src/expression/expr_lambda.rs#L389

I'm working on a fix now!

issues.title: Consider improving `SIM102` and `SIM114` fixes
issues.body:
I originally posted this in https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/23879#issuecomment-4033002209, but these rules look quite compelling on paper (in the documentation), where they suggest diffs like this:

SIM102:

```diff
-if foo:
-    if bar:
-        ...
+if foo and bar:
+    ...
```

SIM114:

```diff
-if x == 1:
-    print("Hello")
-elif x == 2:
+if x == 1 or x == 2:
     print("Hello")
```

However, in more complicated and maybe realistic scenarios, the fix can be less readable:

SIM114:

```diff
-if something in [value1, value2] and another > condition:
-    do_something()
-elif something_else == "a string" and condition:
+if something in [value1, value2] and another > condition or something_else == "a string" and condition:
     do_something()
```

This also makes you think about precedence, and it gets reformatted like this:

```py
if (
    something in [value1, value2]
    and another > condition
    or something_else == "a string"
    and condition
):
    do_something()
```

SIM102 can have the same effect:

```diff
-if something in [value1, value2] and another > condition:
-    if something_else == "a string" and condition:
-        do_something()
+if something in [value1, value2] and another > condition and something_else == "a string" and condition:
+    do_something()
```

(Note that SIM102 checks the `line-length` setting before suggesting this fix, so I ran with `--line-length=180`, if you have trouble reproducing)

One suggestion on #23879 was to make the precedence clear by inserting parentheses in the fixed code. I think another possibility could be not applying the rules to compound conditions, but that might be more controversial.
issues.author: ntBre

comments.body:
I'm less concerned about cases that all use the same operator because precedence is then less of a concern. But I do think parentheses will help a great deal if the fix results in a mix of `and` and `or`. 

The alternative is to rely on https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/parenthesize-chained-operators/ to catch and insert the parentheses.

issues.title: Default ruff check output is extremely slow on large E303-heavy files
issues.body:
### Summary

  ### Summary

  I found a case where plain `ruff check` in the default output mode takes multiple minutes, while the same input is much faster with `--fix`, `--statistics`, and `--output-format concise`.

  I suspect this is in the same general area as diagnostic rendering / reporting performance, but I’m filing it separately because the reproduction here depends on a small number of very large files plus many `E303` diagnostics, and the severity is much
  higher than I expected.

  Potentially related: #7353

  ### Keywords searched

  - `E303 performance`
  - `too-many-blank-lines slow output`
  - `output-format concise performance`
  - `diagnostic rendering performance`

  ### Minimal reproducer

  `E303` is preview-only, so this uses `--preview`.

  ```bash
  python3 -m venv .venv
  . .venv/bin/activate
  python -m pip install "ruff==0.15.10"

  mkdir ruff-e303-large-repro
  cd ruff-e303-large-repro

  cat > generate_case.py <<'PY'
  from pathlib import Path

  root = Path("case")
  root.mkdir()

  chunk = "list[str | int | float | bool | bytes | dict[str, str] | tuple[int, str] | set[int]]"
  anno = " | ".join([chunk] * 60)

  for file_idx in range(6):
      parts = ["from __future__ import annotations\n"]
      for i in range(2500):
          parts.append("\n\n\n")
          parts.append(
              f"def func_{file_idx}_{i}(value_{i}: {anno}) -> {anno}:\n"
              f"    return value_{i}\n"
          )
      (root / f"mod_{file_idx}.py").write_text("".join(parts))

  files = sorted(root.glob("*.py"))
  print("files:", len(files))
  print("total_bytes:", sum(p.stat().st_size for p in files))
  print("largest_bytes:", max(p.stat().st_size for p in files))
  PY

  python generate_case.py
```
  On my machine this produces:

  - 6 files
  - about 150 MB total
  - largest file about 25 MB
  - 15,000 E303 diagnostics

  ### Commands
```bash
  timeout 180s ruff check --isolated --preview --select E303 case
  ruff check --isolated --preview --sel
issues.author: rirze

set[int]] | list[str | int | float | bool | bytes | dict[str, str] | tuple[int, str] | set[int]]:
```

Note the `…` at the end of the line in the diagnostic but the untruncated diff output.

Adding `--unfixable E303` also causes Ruff to finish in ~5 seconds, as I would expect for the above diagnosis.

Thanks for the quick reply. I had to contrive a reasonable approximation of my problem as I experienced it and it took me a few iterations to get something in the ballpark of what I was seeing. 

Your explanation makes sense, it could be related to long lines.

I was a bit hasty to assume that the issue was _rendering_ the diagnostics. After updating the rendering code to truncate, like we do with the diagnostic snippet, the performance wasn't improved. I collected a profile and confirmed that the issue is actually with the diffing itself and not with the output rendering. In other words, I think we're hitting this TODO in the code:

https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/blob/590aab49fcb3c025c172ddd1a9c9c415108c2dfa/crates/ruff_db/src/diagnostic/render/full.rs#L87

We're currently diffing the entire file for each diagnostic rather than narrowing the range to the edited region.
We could consider setting a deadline or experimenting with different algorithms.

Upgrading to similar 3.0.0 or newer might also help:

> Improved Algorithm::Myers performance on heavily unbalanced diffs to avoid pathological slowdowns.
The upgrade alone doesn't seem to have helped, although I didn't let either version run long enough to compare absolute timings. The deadline and other algorithms seem like good ideas to try!

issues.title: Support `tuple` input for decimals
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

The [`Decimal`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/decimal.html#decimal.Decimal) type constructor accepts a three-tuple:

> If value is a [`tuple`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#tuple), it should have three components, a sign (`0` for positive or `1` for negative), a [`tuple`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#tuple) of digits, and an integer exponent. For example, `Decimal((0, (1, 4, 1, 4), -3))` returns `Decimal('1.414')`.

But this isn't accepted by Pydantic, and should probably.

TODO: Update related types documentation after change (currently being rewritten).

### Affected Components

issues.title: Support objects defining `__complex__()` (and others) method for `complex`
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

The [`complex()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#complex) constructor accepts objects defining `__complex__()`, `__float__()` or `__index__()`, but these are not accepted by Pydantic (Which feels inconsistent considering it allows it for floats). Probably we should just delegate directly to the `complex()` constructor? This will most likely be faster that what we do know (a bunch of `isinstance()` calls).

issues.title: Change serialization of fractions in Python mode
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

[Fractions](https://docs.python.org/3/library/fractions.html) are serialized as strings in both Python and JSON mode, which is inconsistent with e.g. decimals.

I think they should be serialized as is in Python mode.

issues.title: Change the default `extra` behavior for dataclasses and TypedDicts
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Following https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10785, this is a feature request tracking the potential change of the default `extra` behavior for TypedDicts and dataclasses.

Samuel mentioned in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10785 that it should be `'forbid'` by default for dataclasses and TypedDicts, but without any motivation.

For dataclasses, my best guess would be that it should mirror the standard library if possible (which doesn't allow extra values). On the other hand, an argument can be made about Pydantic dataclasses and models to behave as closely as possible.

For TypedDicts, things are a bit more unclear (see [PEP 728](https://peps.python.org/pep-0728/) and https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10785#issuecomment-3355959592).

issues.title: Nested update in `model_copy()`
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Allowing something similar to:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Foo(BaseModel):
    param1: int = 1
    param2: int = 2


class Bar(BaseModel):
    param3: int = 3
    param4: Foo = Foo()


bar = Bar()

bar.param4.param2 = 4

bar2 = bar.model_copy(update={"param3": 5, "param4": {"param1": 6}})
# expected: bar2 = Bar(param3=5, param4=Foo(param1=6, param2=4))
```

Looking for appetite/requests for this feature.

issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Generally, we try to encourage users as much as possible to use built-in types (such as `list` or `tuple`) over the abstract collection ones. However, `Sequence` is a pretty common type being used and we can't do much about it (we can't emit warnings for this), and the validation function is currently not that great:

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/effbce64c51c5c7fbf6951b8960dd4e8957a810b/pydantic/_internal/_validators.py#L28-L65

issues.title: Prevent mutations to `model_config` after model definition
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

It is currently perfectly fine at runtime to mutate the `model_config` class attribute for a model. Some users try to do so to e.g. temporarily unfreeze a model:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Model(BaseModel):
    a: int = 1

m = Model()

Model.model_config['frozen'] = False
m.a = 2
Model.model_config['frozen'] = True
```

However, this is an anti-pattern because:
- It only "accidentally" works for some configuration values (e.g. `frozen`), but not all (the model config is inspected in `BaseModel__setattr__()` for `frozen`, but other settings such as [`validate_default`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.validate_default), [`val_temporal_unit`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.val_temporal_unit) etc are affecting validation in `pydantic-core`, where configuration is _not_ read from the model, but rather derived at model build time).
- it is a class variable, and can result to unexpected behavior in multi-threaded environments for instance.
- Since we introduced [caching of `setattr()` handlers](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/10868) in 2.11, the example above doesn't work as expected anymore (see https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12361, https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/11729).

We could use an immutable mapping structure to expose `model_config` (e.g. [`MappingProxyType`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/types.html#types.MappingProxyType)), but this will be breaking.

Before V3, we could set the items of the `ConfigDict` as [`ReadOnly`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#typing.ReadOnly). We might need to differentiate the current `ConfigDict` class (may be used independently, mutated by users before

issues.title: `FieldInfo` 2.12 issues
issues.body:
Some changes in Pydantic 2.12 related to the `FieldInfo` class resulted in issues for libraries relying on this class and its methods. Such changes impact behavior on:
- setting attributes on `FieldInfo` instances
- copying `FieldInfo` instances
- interactions with `FieldInfo` subclasses

While we feel some of these use cases are reasonable and could be better supported, many external libraries are setting too many expectations on the `FieldInfo` logic, and I don't know if we'll be able to fully preserve compatibility for every use case.

## Context

Before diving into the details, I'll give some background on the motivation for these changes. Starting in 2.11, we started refactoring the logic around the `FieldInfo` class,  as it was in a really bad state, and the source of multiple (~10!) separate bugs.

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/11388 (in 2.11) and https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/11898 (in 2.12) were introduced to fix these bugs.

While doing so, we realized many third party libraries were heavily relying on undocumented behavior (e.g. retaining `FieldInfo` subclasses during merging) and some of its methods (e.g. `from_annotation()`, `merge_field_infos()`. This was a mistake from us, we shouldn't have made these public. Most of the bugs that we tried to fix were actually coming from them -- as a result, third party libraries were somewhat relying on bugous behavior).

For reference, here are some examples of inconsistent/bugous behavior, which as a consequence made user logic very brittle:

```python
class ChildFieldInfo(FieldInfo):
    ...

type(ChildFieldInfo.from_annotation(typing.Annotated[typing.Any, ChildFieldInfo()]))
#> ChildFieldInfo
type(ChildFieldInfo.from_annotation(typing.Annotated[typing.Any, ChildFieldInfo(), ChildFieldInfo()]))
#> FieldInfo, subclass not preserved

# ---

f: FieldInfo = ...

f_copy = copy.copy(f)

f_copy.metadata.append(...)  # Affects `f` as well

# ---

# In 2.11:

class Model(BaseModel):
    f: int

f =
issues.author: Viicos

comments.body:
I was using FieldInfo class to construct a "patch" version of a model. The logic was to set defaults of all fields to None and filter out them later with `exclude_unset` getting only those keys and values that a user intended to update.

```py
from pydantic.fields import FieldInfo
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, create_model

class TestModel(BaseModel):
    name: str

def create_patch_model(cls: type[BaseModel]) -> type[BaseModel]:
    fields = {}
    for field_name, field in cls.model_fields.items():
        field_copy: FieldInfo = field._copy()
        field_copy.default = None
        fields[field_name] = (field.annotation, field_copy)
    return create_model(f'Patch{cls.__name__}', **fields)

print(create_patch_model(TestModel).model_fields['name'].is_required())
print(create_patch_model(TestModel).model_validate({}))
```

For some reason, using 2.12 outputs that name here is required and thus the last line raises validation exception. An identical approach worked without any issues in 2.11.

I could not figure out how to implement the same behaviour with 2.12: I wish to preserve all the metadata of a field in original model and only update the default value, forcing Pydantic to treat field as non-required. I pinned the version to <2.12 by now.

I have created the same issue (similar) without searching first. It has affected our way of creating dynamic models via `create_model` too. Here's my issue and MRE (please check it too): #12380.

Our use-case: dynamic creation of `pydantic` models via `create_model`. We do it this way to use `pydantic`'s validation and `pydantic`'s features for validation of incoming and outcoming data and heavily really on this.

PS:
It was really hard to debug this too, because I saw `FieldInfo` repr and just copied it to another file, but the result was different in that case (no bug, since there were no dynamic change of `default` field)...

For the part above, I can't give MRE, unfortunately, but I had situation like this:
```python
# fields used when using `create_model`
fields={'namespace': (typing.Optional[str], FieldInfo(annotation=Union[str, NoneType], required=False, default=None))}

# result model
{'namespace': FieldInfo(annotation=Union[str, NoneType], required=True)}
```
If it helps, I think I will be able to provide an MRE for this too. But it was really unpleasant to copy `repr `to another file and get different results...

I have a similar problem.

Use case: remove all defaults from a (possibly nested) Pydantic model

This code stopped working as expected. I have a feeling that `create_model` no longer overrides fields in the `__base__` provided:

```python
from copy import deepcopy

from pydantic import BaseModel, create_model
from pydantic_core import PydanticUndefined


def strip_defaults_recursive[T: BaseModel](model: type[T]) -> type[T]:
    original_fields = model.model_fields
    new_fields = {}

    for name, info in original_fields.items():
        new_info = deepcopy(info)

        if (new_info.default is not PydanticUndefined) or (
            new_info.default_factory is not None
        ):
            new_info.default = PydanticUndefined
            new_info.default_factory = None

        if isinstance(new_info.annotation, type) and issubclass(
            new_info.annotation, BaseModel
        ):
            new_class = strip_defaults_recursive(new_info.annotation)
            new_info.annotation = new_class

        new_fields[name] = (new_info.annotation, new_info)

    new_model = create_model(
        model.__name__,
        __base__=model,
        **new_fields,
    )
    return new_model
```

Thanks for the provided feedback. Altering `FieldInfo` instances only accidentally worked in 2.11 under certain scenarios (see an example in my OP where mutations are _not_ preserved in <=2.11).

For the next patch release, I'll see if I can fix the mutation logic so that it is consistently taken into account. I may also consider publicly exposing the copy logic. However, note that I _don't_ recommend going this way. Although it technically works, using a `FieldInfo` instance as `Annotated` metadata/an assignment/default to a field isn't expected by Pydantic. The recommended approach is option 1 in my OP (where we need to provide some utilities to help implementing this approach in user code).

@Viicos thanks for reading this!

I would like to clarify.

1. So we should expect to **NOT** be able to:
1.a. Set attributes dinamically on `FieldInfo`
1.b. Use `copy` or `deepcopy` on `FieldInfo`

2. Basically, we should treat `FieldInfo` as frozen dataclass, correct?

3. And we should change our code base, not to expect `pydantic` to change anything.

Is it correct?
Because for me it seems pretty strange that there's such a massive change in behavior that was stable for 2 years.

@Viicos , also, as I tested, mutations were possible and were respected in all version from `2.2.0` (Aug 17, 2023) to `2.11.10` (Oct 4, 2025). So this was the established behavior for more then 2 years.

Please check my issue: #12380

@Danipulok, ideally yes `FieldInfo` structures should be treated as immutable, but in practice it worked fine under some circumstances, so we need to be pragmatic and try to support this use case as much as we can.

> mutations were possible and were respected in all version

As I mentioned, see the example in my OP that shows that mutations were _not_ always respected (mutations are also tracked in an internal `_attributes_set` that we need to get rid with, see https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/11388#issue-2830816968 for more context).

More generally (as per the context section in the OP), the logic around the `FieldInfo` class has been really messy for a long time, and we had to make some fixes/changes to fix many bugs around it. In an ideal world, these bugs are fixed without breaking existing usage, but in reality this is far from trivial.

@Viicos 

> so we need to be pragmatic and try to support this use case as much as we can.

> In an ideal world, these bugs are fixed without breaking existing usage, but in reality this is far from trivial.

Got it. So the code base from projects that use `FieldInfo` should be changed and treat `FieldInfo` as immutable class to prevent bugs and allow support for more `pydantic` versions. Thank you!

@Viicos, hey again, sorry for pinging
While I'm here (fixing our code-base), could you please tell me why is `FieldInfo` not in `__all__` ?
It seems to be pretty stable and makes sense to make it public (only for people who know what they are doing, IMHO)

Since there's no way to initialize `FieldInfo` with `annotation` and other attributes at the same time and to omit dynamically changing attributes, the single option I see is to directly initialize it.

Also, what about raising errors when trying to set dynamic attributes to `FieldInfo`?
Because for me, it was really frustrating to have something "working" (I checked it using `repr`), when in reality it was not

> While I'm here (fixing our code-base), could you please tell me why is `FieldInfo` not in `__all__` ?

We started exposing it in the `pydantic.fields`'s `__all__` attribute in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12339, so it can properly be imported from `pydantic.fields`.

> It seems to be pretty stable and makes sense to make it public (only for people who know what they are doing, IMHO)

As I mention in the OP, the `FieldInfo` class was (and is partially still is) in a really bad state, so we'll probably consider exposing it in the root `pydantic` module only when we cleared everything.

> Also, what about raising errors when trying to set dynamic attributes to `FieldInfo`?

What are you referring to by "dynamic attributes"? Is it known attributes but set via direct assignment or unknown/user defined `FieldInfo` attributes? For the former, as I said I will try to properly support this pattern (without the `_attributes_set` logic).

@Viicos 

> We started exposing it in the pydantic.fields's __all__ attribute in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12339, so it can properly be imported from pydantic.fields.

That's really nice! Thank you

> As I mention in the OP, the FieldInfo class was (and is partially still is) in a really bad state, so we'll probably consider exposing it in the root pydantic module only when we cleared everything.

Seems pretty logical

> What are you referring to by "dynamic attributes"? Is it known attributes but set via direct assignment or unknown/user defined FieldInfo attributes? For the former, as I said I will try to properly support this pattern (without the _attributes_set logic).

I just rewrote the code to pass the variables at the `__init__` and everything worked. Before that, we set some attributes on already created field, because we used `is_required` property to check if field is required on `pydantic` side

Update: I just opened https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12411 which should address most issues described here.

- **I managed to restore the existing (<=2.11) behavior**. Provided you make a deep copy of the `FieldInfo` instance [^1], you can make direct assignments and expect it to be picked up when using the `FieldInfo` instance as `Annotated` metadata. This means that **you don't need to worry about `_attributes_set`** anymore (cc @glatterf42, @zmievsa, @abrookins (sorry for the tag again), @buhrmann, @tarsil).

    ```python
    fields = {}

    field_copy: FieldInfo = deepcopy(field)
    field_copy.default = None
    fields[field_name] = (field.annotation, field_copy)   # This works again

    create_model(..., **fields)
    ```

- I added a [documentation example](https://fieldinfo-fixes-2-12.pydantic-docs.pages.dev/examples/dynamic_models) showing the _expected_ way of defining models from existing ones (whether it makes every field optional/required, adds aliases, etc). It does _not_ involve copying `FieldInfo` instances, nor making direct mutations on them, but rather leverages a new `FieldInfo.asdict()` method. This example is **the only supported way** of achieving this use case, and I can only encourage you using it (of course, I'd be happy to get feedback on whether this suits your needs). The approach of mutating and passing the `FieldInfo` instance back as `Annotated` metadata may break/be deprecated at any time.
- I undocumented various `FieldInfo` methods, such as `from_annotation()` and `from_annotated_attribute()`. This doesn't mean we make them entirely private and will make breaking changes on them as we like, but given external usage of such methods doesn't really make sense (and they were kind of misused anyway -- they are meant to convert from the original type annotation and optionally assignment to a final `FieldInfo` instance, but only external usage I encountered was passing `FieldInfo` instances as arguments), I prefer to avoid advertising them as tools that could be useful for external consumers. 
- For `FieldInfo` subclasses, I unfortunately can't do much. As I mention in the OP, I can only _strongly_ encourage favoring composition instead of inheritance. Do not hesitate to share your use case and we can discuss alternatives.

[^1]: we have a private `FieldInfo._copy()` method that we use internally, but I'd rather not make it public unless there's a valid use case to do so.
I just published [2.12.3](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/releases/tag/v2.12.3) to PyPI, which includes the above. Closing as such.

issues.title: Serializer for extra fields not used
issues.body:
From https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1835/files#r2425738743:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Model(BaseModel, extra='allow'):
    a: int
    __pydantic_extra__: dict[str, int]


m = Model(a=1, extra=1)
m.a = 'not_an_int'
m.model_dump_json()  # PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue warning raised

m.a = 1
m.extra = 'not_an_int'
m.model_dump_json()  # No warning raised
```

Also https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1835/files#r2425737760 should probably be supported.

### Initial Checks

### Description

Follow up to https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/11748

Thanks so much for rolling out a fix so quickly for the original bug 🙏 

Unfortunately it seems that the bug is still there as soon as we change the original MRE to make the `Other` model class inherit from another model 🥲 

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Generic, Optional, TypeVar
from pydantic import BaseModel


class ConfiguredBaseModel(BaseModel):
    """Base model to share configuration settings."""
    # example model_config commented out since the bug seems present with or
    # without the model_config set

    # model_config = ConfigDict(
    #     extra="ignore",
    #     revalidate_instances="always",
    #     validate_assignment=True,
    # )


T = TypeVar("T")


class Base(ConfiguredBaseModel, Generic[T]):
    t: Optional[T]


class Other(ConfiguredBaseModel): # if we change this to inherit from BaseModel then the model validates as expected
    children: "Base[Other]"


a = Other.model_validate({"children": {"t": {"children": {"t": None}}}})

print(a)

issues.title: Deferred imports failure with Python 3.14
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Pydantic 2.12 fails to resolve deferred imports in Python 3.14
See the example below.

Notes:
* reproduced with Python 3.14.0
* not reproduced with Python 3.13.2
* not reproduced with Python 3.14.0rc2 !
* issue disappears if we remove `from __future__ import annotations` in the example below
* issue disappears if `C.c` field is of type `A` instead of `B` in the example below

This issue was discovered in LangChain: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/pull/33560#issuecomment-3415054441

### Example Code

In file a.py

```python
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import TypedDict, Required

class A(TypedDict):
    x: Required[int]
```

in file b.py

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from a import A

class B(A):
    pass

class C(BaseModel):
    c: B

C.model_validate({"c": {"x": 1}})
```

then run b.py

```
python b.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/private/tmp/b.py", line 10, in <module>
    C.model_validate({"c": {"x": 1}})
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

issues.title: Fields with `exclude_if` should not be required in serialization JSON schema
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I've been happily using the new `exclude_if` feature, but it's not quite behaving as expected.

Expectation: the JSON schema in serialization mode marks fields with `exclude_if` as optional. The JSON schema accurately describes the structure of the serialization output.

Reality: such fields are marked as required, even though they may be absent in the actual output.

For example, consider this model:

```python
class MyModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
    field: Annotated[str, pydantic.Field(exclude_if=lambda x: x == "null")]
```

Its input and output JSON schema are as follows, with the `field` marked as `required`:

```json5
{"properties": {"field": {"title": "Field", "type": "string"}},
 "required": ["field"],  // <--
 "title": "MyModel",
 "type": "object"}
```

However, dumping values like `MyModel(field="null")` would results in an empty dict `{}` which violates the promised schema. Given the model, I'd have expected the `field` to be required in validation mode, but optional in serialization mode.

This matters when using a code-first design for a JSON schema, e.g. when writing FastAPI applications. A potential workaround is to set a default value, but that's not quite equivalent.

I used the below script to inspect schemas for for BaseModels and Dataclasses. In its output, I would expect the `'required': ['field']` item to be present in the input schemas but absent in the output schemas. It could perhaps make sense to include schema-level tests for the Exclude-If feature, in addition to the existing serialization tests ([models](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/3154a95fbd442b02612561d91347eebf48fba79a/tests/test_main.py#L1389-L1404), [dataclasses](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/3154a95fbd442b02612561d91347eebf48fba79a/tests/test_dataclasses.py#L3244-L3258)).

(Anecdotally, I've also seen warnings like `Expected 3 fields but got 2` as triggered by [this Pydantic-Cor
issues.author: latk

>>> test_exclude_model_json_schema()
{'properties': {'field': {'title': 'Field', 'type': 'integer'}}, 'required': ['field'], 'title': 'MyModel', 'type': 'object'}

issues.title: `FieldInfo` not expanding parameterization
issues.body:
```python
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class Model[T](BaseModel):
    t: T

M = Model[Annotated[int, Field(gt=1)]]

M.model_fields['t']
#> FieldInfo(annotation=Annotated[int, FieldInfo(annotation=NoneType, required=True, metadata=[Gt(gt=1)])], required=True)
```

Related: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/11922.
issues.author: Viicos

comments.body:
Hi 👋

I’ve reproduced this issue and verified that the root cause is that FieldInfo from Annotated arguments isn’t being expanded when used in a parameterized GenericModel.

I implemented a small fix that ensures the behavior matches non-generic models:

```
M = Model[Annotated[int, Field(gt=1)]]
print(M.model_fields['t'].metadata)
# Before: []
# After fix: [Gt(gt=1)]
```

So the metadata propagation now works consistently between generic and regular models.

The fix adds expansion of Annotated inside parameterized typevars and merges their FieldInfo/metadata into the final model field.

If this direction looks correct, I can open a PR (with tests and comments) so you can review the patch.

Thanks!
@August26 Feel free to give it a shot, I'll note that it might be not trivial.

issues.title: deep-copying `MISSING` crashes in `BaseModel.model_construct()` and `BaseModel.__deepcopy__()` (TypeError: Cannot pickle 'Sentinel' object)
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

## Problem
When a field’s default is the experimental `MISSING` sentinel (pydantic >=2.12), Pydantic ends up trying to deepcopy that sentinel in a few places. The sentinel isn’t deepcopy/pickle-friendly, so this raises: 

> TypeError: Cannot pickle 'Sentinel' object.

## Where it happens
- `BaseModel.model_construct()`: default resolution goes through `smart_deepcopy()`, which currently tries to deepcopy `MISSING`.
- `BaseModel.__deepcopy__()`: stdlib `deepcopy()` is called on `self.__dict__` (and others), which hits `MISSING` and fails.

## Proposed fix
1) In `smart_deepcopy()`, return early for the sentinel:

```python
# pydantic/_internal/_utils.py
from pydantic_core import MISSING

def smart_deepcopy(obj):
    if obj is MISSING:
        return obj
    # rest of current logic...
```

This keeps the singleton by identity and avoids deepcopy entirely.

2) In `BaseModel.__deepcopy__()`, use `smart_deepcopy()` (with memo) instead of raw `deepcopy()` for model internals.

- Today it does `deepcopy(self.__dict__, memo=...)`; that will crash if values contain `MISSING`.
- `smart_deepcopy()` doesn’t accept memo yet, it could be extended to do so and thread it into the fallback deepcopy call.

## Rationale
- `MISSING` is a singleton sentinel meant to be passed by identity. Copying it isn’t useful and currently fails.
- Using `smart_deepcopy()` in `BaseModel.__deepcopy__()` aligns behavior between construction and copying and avoids duplicating special cases.

## Minimal repro
```python
from copy import deepcopy
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from pydantic.experimental.missing_sentinel import MISSING  # 2.12.x

class M(BaseModel):
    a: int | MISSING = Field(default=MISSING)

# 1) model_construct path
M.model_construct()  # attempts to deepcopy MISSING -> TypeError

# 2) __deepcopy__ path
m = M()
deepcopy(m)          # deepcopy(self.__dict__) hits MISSING -> TypeError
```

Expected
- B
issues.author: ornariece

comments.body: This is expected, as per [the documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/experimental/#missing-sentinel), PEP 661 sentinels aren't pickleable. We can update `smart_deepcopy()` to account for this in the meanwhile, but this won't solve all issue.

What do you think of my proposed tweak to `smart_deepcopy()`? And using `smart_deepcopy()` in `.__deepcopy__()`? @Viicos 
I'm happy to draft a PR :).
A PR is welcomed but only to tweak `smart_deepcopy()`, I'd rather not change the current `__deepcopy__()` behavior

issues.title: Excessive memory usage for regex validators
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

After upgrading pydantic from version 2.10.6 to 2.11, we noticed a large (4x) increase in startup memory usage in our fastapi app. It seems to be related to regex validator creation. Example 1 demonstrates high memory usage. We were able to mitigate the issue by refactoring to example 2 which does not suffer from the issue.

It seems like the issue is specific to some aspects of this regex. Changing the regex makes it mostly go away. So it seems like there are two things going on here: This particular regex takes up a lot of memory and it is replicated in memory due to how it is used by pydantic.

Memray stats for example 1:
Total number of allocations: 4084096
Total number of frames seen: 0
Peak memory usage: 307.6 MB
Python allocator: pymalloc

memray stats for example 2:
Total number of allocations: 47961
Total number of frames seen: 0
Peak memory usage: 7.2 MB
Python allocator: pymalloc

### Example Code

```Python
Example 1:

from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError

ConstraintType = Annotated[str, Field(pattern="^(\\p{L}|_)(\\p{L}|\\p{N}|[.\\-_])*$")]


class TestModel1(BaseModel):
# 400 instances of the type help highlight the poor scaling behavior
    field_1: ConstraintType
    field_2: ConstraintType
    ...
    field_400: ConstraintType

==================================================================
Example 2:

from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, RootModel


class RootType(RootModel[str]):

    root: Annotated[
        str,
        Field(
            pattern="^(\\p{L}|_)(\\p{L}|\\p{N}|[.\\-_])*$"
        ),
    ]

class TestModel2(BaseModel):
    field_1: RootType
    field_2: RootType    
    ...
    field_400: RootType

```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.12.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.41.5
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
issues.author: dzaslavskiy

comments.body:
I get roughly the same numbers as you, but can also reproduce on 2.10. Are you sure you didn't have the issue in 2.10?

Memory usage seem to depend greatly on the complexity of the regex pattern.

In any case, we should cache the compiled regex patterns from the `regex` crate in core.

I agree that it can be reproduced in 2.10.6. I looks like something else changed then that just caused us to notice the regex memory usage. I will try to narrow down to the root cause. Here are the stats for the actual code:

pydantic 2.10.6
Duration: 0:00:42.119000
Total number of allocations: 12118721
Total number of frames seen: 0
Peak memory usage: 1.4 GB
Python allocator: pymalloc debug

pydantic 2.12.0
Duration: 0:03:06.126000
Total number of allocations: 53498679
Total number of frames seen: 0
Peak memory usage: 4.0 GB
Python allocator: pymalloc debug

Can you help me understand what makes example 2 above (RootModel) work fine without regex caching in contrast to example 1?

> I will try to narrow down to the root cause.

Thanks, it looks like something else might be causing the memory increase, really interested in knowing what could be the issue.

---

> Can you help me understand what makes example 2 above (RootModel) work fine without regex caching in contrast to example 1?

In example 1, `ConstraintType` is an "implicit" type alias. At runtime, the code is equivalent to:

```python
class TestModel1(BaseModel):
# 400 instances of the type help highlight the poor scaling behavior
    field_1: Annotated[str, Field(pattern="^(\\p{L}|_)(\\p{L}|\\p{N}|[.\\-_])*$")]
    field_2: Annotated[str, Field(pattern="^(\\p{L}|_)(\\p{L}|\\p{N}|[.\\-_])*$")]
    ...
    field_400: Annotated[str, Field(pattern="^(\\p{L}|_)(\\p{L}|\\p{N}|[.\\-_])*$")]
```

When building models, Pydantic builds a core schema (a dict structure) derived from the annotations, describing how validation and serialization should be performed. In this example, a lot of duplication happens because the core schema is being built 400 times (and the validator objects from `pydantic-core` as well, in particular the compiled regex patterns).

As of today, Pydantic apply some really basic caching logic for validators and serializers (e.g. if you use `str` as a plain annotation, we reuse the validator for this type, but not currently for types with constraints). This is something we are aiming for.

In example 2, you are using a root model, which is a _referenceable_ type, meaning it will appear as a definition in the core schema for `TestModel2`:

```python
{
│   'type': 'definitions',
│   'schema': {
│   │   'type': 'model',
│   │   'cls': <class '__main__.TestModel2'>,
│   │   'schema': {
│   │   │   'type': 'model-fields',
│   │   │   'fields': {
│   │   │   │   'field_1': {'type': 'model-field', 'schema': {'type': 'definition-ref', 'schema_ref': '__main__.RootType:933267872'}, 'metadata': {}},
│   │   │   │   'field_2': {'type': 'model-field', 'schema': {'type': 'definition-ref', 'schema_ref': '__main__.RootType:933267872'}, 'metadata': {}},
│   │   │   │   'field_400': {'type': 'model-field', 'schema': {'type': 'definition-ref', 'schema_ref': '__main__.RootType:933267872'}, 'metadata': {}}
│   │   │   },
│   │   │   'model_name': 'TestModel2',
│   │   │   'computed_fields': []
│   │   },
│   │   'config': {'title': 'TestModel2'},
│   │   'ref': '__main__.TestModel2:933288448',
│   │   'metadata': {'<stripped>'}
│   },
│   'definitions': [
│   │   {
│   │   │   'type': 'model',
│   │   │   'cls': <class '__main__.RootType'>,
│   │   │   'schema': {'type': 'str', 'pattern': '^(\\p{L}|_)(\\p{L}|\\p{N}|[.\\-_])*$'},
│   │   │   'root_model': True,
│   │   │   'config': {'title': 'RootType'},
│   │   │   'ref': '__main__.RootType:933267872',
│   │   │   'metadata': {'<stripped>'}
│   │   }
│   ]
}
```

Similarly, the validator/serializer is only being built once for the root model. An (somewhat cleaner/simpler) alternative is to use an [explicit type alias](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/types/#named-type-aliases):

```python
type ConstraintType = Annotated[str, Field(pattern="^(\\p{L}|_)(\\p{L}|\\p{N}|[.\\-_])*$")]
```

issues.title: Altering Pydantic behavior/configuration globally
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Currently, Pydantic behavior can generally be controlled using [the configuration](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/config/). Pydantic is quite configurable: validation/serialization behavior, JSON Schema, behavior for specific types, etc. But this also means that we have to choose reasonable defaults for all these configuration values.

As an application/library author, you often want to set your own defaults, to be used in your entire code base. The canonical way to do so is to [define your own `BaseModel`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/config/#change-behaviour-globally):

```python
class MyBaseModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = {'validate_by_name': True}
```

However, there are limitations with this approach:

- [Configuration is _not_ propagated](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/config/#configuration-propagation) for Pydantic model and dataclasses.
- If you happen to use dataclasses, TypedDicts or `TypeAdapter`, you need specify this configuration every time.
- For some settings, it makes sense to have it applied globally (e.g. [`ignored_types`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.ignored_types)).

---

It may be beneficial for end users to have a way to set the configuration globally. I believe this is particularly useful for applications (e.g. a FastAPI backend app). Unlike libraries, you "own" all of your code base. For instance, the [Django REST Framework](https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework) libraries (and Django in general) uses [settings](https://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/settings/) to control the behavior globally, where you can provide your own defaults that can be overridden per viewset. In some way, Pydantic is no different when u

issues.title: Do not make `PydanticUserError` a `TypeError` subclass

issues.title: String's case conversion using `LowerCase` and `UpperCase` from `annotated-types` not working
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

According to [documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/standard_library_types/#strings), `LowerCase` and `UpperCase` from `annotated-types` can be used for `str`. However, the string's case remain unchanged. `StringConstraints` works as expected.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Annotated

from annotated_types import LowerCase, UpperCase
from pydantic import BaseModel, StringConstraints

test_string = "Test String"

# This works
class StrWithStringConstraints(BaseModel):
    upper_case: Annotated[str, StringConstraints(to_upper=True)]
    lower_case: Annotated[str, StringConstraints(to_lower=True)]

print(StrWithStringConstraints(upper_case=test_string, lower_case=test_string))
# output: upper_case='TEST STRING' lower_case='test string'

# This is not working
class StrWithAnnotatedTypes(BaseModel):
    upper_case: Annotated[str, UpperCase]
    lower_case: Annotated[str, LowerCase]

print(StrWithAnnotatedTypes(upper_case=test_string, lower_case=test_string))
# output: upper_case='Test String' lower_case='Test String'
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.12.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.41.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
               python version: 3.13.5 (main, Jun 25 2025, 18:55:22) [GCC 14.2.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2-x86_64-with-glibc2.41
             related packages: fastapi-0.120.0 pydantic-extra-types-2.10.6 pydantic-settings-2.11.0 typing_extensions-4.15.0 email-validator-2.3.0

issues.author: wesley312

comments.body: As per the `annotated_types` [documentation](https://github.com/annotated-types/annotated-types?tab=readme-ov-file#predicate), these are implemented as predicates, so you should use them like `upper_case: UpperCase[str]`. Note that they are different from `to_upper` and `to_lower`. The latter will convert them to upper/lower case, while the latter will just raise a validation error if they are not upper/lower case.
