issues.title: No ForwardRef in model_fields annotations with list['Model'] field type with legacy-style annotations (w/o from __future__ import annotations)
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

There is no `ForwardRef` in model_fields annotations  with `list['Model']` field type with legacy-style annotations (w/o `from __future__ import annotations`) when forward model name is declared as string literal.

So `model_rebuild()` does not work correctly, and after rebuild there is no actual type in field annotation.

If we take a look into captured annotations, we'll see that event before rebuild annotation is not a `ForwardRef`.

```py
class Foo(BaseModel):
    bar1: 'Bar'
    bar2: list['Bar']


class Bar(BaseModel):
    pass


print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # ForwardRef('Bar') OK
print(Foo.model_fields['bar2'].annotation)  # list['Bar'] ???

Foo.model_rebuild()

print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # <class '__main__.Bar'> OK
print(Foo.model_fields['bar2'].annotation)  # list['Bar'] ???
```

with `from __future__ import annotations` PEP563 annotations everything works as expected

```py
class Foo(BaseModel):
    bar1: Bar
    bar2: list[Bar]


class Bar(BaseModel):
    pass


print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # ForwardRef('Bar') OK
print(Foo.model_fields['bar2'].annotation)  # ForwardRef('list[Bar]') OK

Foo.model_rebuild()

print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # <class '__main__.Bar'> OK
print(Foo.model_fields['bar2'].annotation)  # list[__main__.Bar] OK
```

### Example Code

```Python
# from __future__ import annotations
import sys

import pydantic
from pydantic import BaseModel

print(sys.version)  # 3.10.4 (tags/v3.10.4:9d38120, Mar 23 2022, 23:13:41) [MSC v.1929 64 bit (AMD64)]
print(pydantic.version.VERSION)  # 2.12.5


class Foo(BaseModel):
    bar1: 'Bar'
    bar2: list['Bar']


class Bar(BaseModel):
    pass


print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # ForwardRef('Bar') OK
print(Foo.model_fields['bar2'].annotation)  # list['Bar'] ???
print()
Foo.model_rebuild()
print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # <class '__main__.Bar'>

comments.body:
This is due to a CPython bug (https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/30900), present in 3.10 and lower. [We are aware of it](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/11759#issuecomment-2808852424), and I'd like to see if I can fix it but only after we drop Python 3.9, so that I can clean some parts of the evaluation logic first.

Note that while the field info annotation is still containing a forward ref, it is actually evaluated during core schema generation.

issues.title: Premature warning about Python 3.14 in pydantic.v1
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When a module is imported `from pydantic.v1` and a user is running Python 3.14, they'll get a warning: "Core Pydantic V1 functionality isn't compatible with Python 3.14 or greater."

On the surface, the logic here is sound: if the user is importing something from `pydantic.v1` they must be about to use that thing, so let's warn them that it's not going to work.

But there are packages that import classes from V1 just to _check_ if the end user is using V1. For example, [LangChain in this file](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/libs/core/langchain_core/utils/pydantic.py).

In these cases, the warning is confusing and sends developers down a rabbit hole of trying to work out "who's trying to use Pydantic V1?" and if they don't know Python intimately, they might think that different packages are using different versions of Pydantic at the same time and they're clashing, all rather unpleasant, considering that nothing's actually wrong!

I see three possible futures:
1. Python 3.14 users in this situation get warnings, they can ignore them (warning fatigue, not great)
2. All packages that do this "import from `pydantic.v1` to check usage" pattern fix their code to check in some way that doesn't involve importing from `pydantic.v1` (probably not going to happen)
3. Pydantic refines the logic to only show this warning when it looks like something is actually going to break (e.g. triggered in the constructors of classes)

The current logic is going to be helpful to a shrinking number of users, and rather annoying for a growing number of users.

Relevant PR: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12263

### Example Code

```Python

```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.12.5
        pydantic-core version: 2.41.5
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
               python version: 3.14.0 (main, Nov 19 2025, 22:43:52) [MSC v.19
issues.author: davidgilbertson

comments.body: As mentioned in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12263's body, I wasn't really satisfied with the warning for the reasons you mention. I'm not sure what's the best path forward here, I'll need to think about it.
I managed to add initial support for python 3.14 in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12636, which will be released in a new version soon.
https://pypi.org/project/pydantic/1.10.25/ was just published.

> https://pypi.org/project/pydantic/1.10.25/ was just published.

Hi, I know this is out of context, but what exactly will or can be supported in Python 3.14+ when using the `pydantic.v1` layer from Pydantic 2+?
Or more bluntly, is it possible at all that the changes brought by new Python versions be overcome/worked-around in the `.v1` modules, only within the actual latest Pydantic package?
Thank you!

Not sure I understand your question, but we backport the latest v1 version in the `pydantic.v1` module, on each minor v2 release (next one is 2.13).

In this case, Pydantic 2.13's `v1` module will contain the `v1.10.26` version of Pydantic v1, which has support for Python 3.14.
Great work, thanks @Viicos !

> we backport the latest v1 version in the pydantic.v1 module, on each minor v2 release (next one is 2.13).
>
> In this case, Pydantic 2.13's v1 module will contain the v1.10.26 version of Pydantic v1, which has support for Python 3.14.

@Viicos  Do you have a rough ETA for this?

(We are currently rebuilding all Python packages on Arch Linux against Python 3.14 and have a few `pydantic.v1` consumers that break, which blocks the rebuild.)
We initially wanted to make a beta 2.13 release once we get https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12518 merged, but I can look into making a beta release earlier (between Wednesday and Sunday).

Thanks! much appreciated!

In the meantime I've added a backported patch for the v1 module, but I'm not sure if that is all or whether this will introduce other issues for the consumers: https://gitlab.archlinux.org/archlinux/packaging/packages/python-pydantic/-/commit/2b567d3d5ebfa95a68b00e20f6b304b8186c8ce7
(all will be in testing on our end for quite some time I guess anyhow).
Due to limited availability, I will probably do the beta release early next week instead.
I just released [v2.13.0b1](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/releases/tag/v2.13.0b1), which includes the latest V1 version with Python 3.14 support. Final release will follow in one or two weeks.
@Viicos Thank you for your great work! Is there already a plan for the next release?
No planned date, but we're quite close (a couple fixes to include still).

issues.title: `TypeAdapter.dump_json` slower than orjson seemingly due to number of arguments passed to pydantic-core
issues.body:
I ran the following code (tested with Pydantic 2.11):

```python
from timeit import timeit

from orjson import dumps as to_json_orjson
from pydantic import TypeAdapter
from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class Example:
    b: str

ta = TypeAdapter(Example)
to_json_pydantic = ta.dump_json

e = Example(b='1q23')

print(to_json_orjson(e))
print(to_json_pydantic(e))

N = 1000000
pydantic = timeit('to_json_pydantic(e)', globals=globals(), number=N)
orjson = timeit('to_json_orjson(e)', globals=globals(), number=N)

print(f'Pydantic: {pydantic:.3f}s')
print(f'orjson: {orjson:.3f}s')
```

On my M1 mac, I find that `orjson` is about 2x faster, and running a profile the main cost in the Pydantic code seems to be inside PyO3's argument parsing.

I think it's probably just the sheer number of keyword arguments passed to `SchemaSerializer.to_json`. I wonder if this should be optimized in PyO3, pydantic, or both.

Aside, I am sure that I previously tested this same sample on a Linux desktop and found orjson to be 10x faster, but I need to re-check that result and understand if that was really the case.

issues.title: `after` validator run twice
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The following code produces two calls of the `after` validator for A, this seems like a bug.

### Example Code

```Python
from collections import defaultdict
from typing import Self

from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validator

COUNTER = defaultdict(lambda: 0)


class A(BaseModel):
    x: int

    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def running_validation(self) -> Self:
        COUNTER['A'] += 1
        return self


class B(BaseModel):
    a: A

    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def running_validation(self) -> Self:
        COUNTER['B'] += 1
        return self


B(a=A(x=2))
print(COUNTER)  # prints {'A': 2, 'B': 1}
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
Pydantic 2.11
```

issues.title: Environment variable to show full input value in errors
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

See validation error like this, which raises a `ValidationError`:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError
import json

class SampleModel(BaseModel):
    id: int
    name: str

# Create a large but invalid JSON input (e.g., missing required fields, wrong types)
invalid_json = json.dumps({
    "id": 123 * 1e10,
    "data": ["a"] * 10000  # Large array to increase size
})

try:
    obj = SampleModel.model_validate_json(invalid_json)
except ValidationError as e:
    print("Validation error:", e)
```

The `ValidationError` has truncated input value to avoid bloating exception messages:

```
Validation error: 1 validation error for SampleModel
name
  Field required [type=missing, input_value={'id': 1230000000000.0, '...a', 'a', 'a', 'a', 'a']}, input_type=dict]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.11/v/missing
```

I propose we add an environment variable, `PYDANTIC_SHOW_FULL_ERRORS=1` (to be bikeshed) which stops the truncation, so the user can more easily debug. 

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [x] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)

- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)

issues.author: davidhewitt

comments.body: This would be the first public environment variable, so probably we'll need to figure out how/where to properly document it.

What is the motivation for an ENV var instead of capturing this in ConfigDict? 

There's already config items that are related (e.g, hide_input_in_errors,  validation_error_cause).

> What the motivation for an ENV var instead of capturing this in ConfigDict? 
> 
> There's already config items that are related (e.g, hide_input_in_errors,  validation_error_cause).

This feature would only make sense for debugging purposes and would not be suitable for production use. The other configuration items you mentioned can have a real use in application logic.

> This feature would only make sense for debugging purposes and would not be suitable for production use. The other configuration items you mentioned can have a real use in application logic.

Sure, but once it's a public API, it's hard to enforce or even suggest that it should only be used in a specific context. 

I suspect `PYDANTIC_SHOW_FULL_ERRORS` + `FailFast` could be a useful combination in a production setting. 

It might also be useful in context of BaseSettings and a dotenv file.
We have `PYDANTIC_ERRORS_INCLUDE_URL` which is semi-public; it's documented on `ValidationError.__repr__`.
Xref #12556

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

Split from #9571

For `datetime` validation & serialization we should consider allowing an IANA timezone when provided in the form specified by [RFC 9557](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9557.html#name-examples).

issues.title: (Typed) dictionaries and `serialize_as_any` behavior
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Related: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/3427.

It seems to me that using `SerializeAsAny`/`serialize_as_any` means "treat the field annotation as `Any` when serializing a field value". However, setting `extra='allow'` is necessary to make this work, but I'm not sure this would be necessary?

```python
from typing import TypedDict

from pydantic import BaseModel, SerializeAsAny, with_config

# @with_config(extra='allow')
class Base(TypedDict):
    foo: str


# Equivalent to subclassing Base, as TDs are structural types
class Sub(TypedDict):
    foo: str
    bar: int


class Model(BaseModel):
    x: SerializeAsAny[Base]

m = Model(x=Sub(foo="hello", bar=123))

m.model_dump()
#> {'x': {'foo': 'hello'}}
```

If we uncomment the `with_config` on `Base`, it properly serializes as `Any`, but looks like it shouldn't be required?

cc @DouweM 

### Example Code

```Python

```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
2.11
```
issues.author: Viicos

comments.body:
It looks like the issue is not with `SerializeAsAny`, but rather that validation is removing the `bar` value because it is not a valid field on `Base`:

```python
from typing import TypedDict

from pydantic import BaseModel, SerializeAsAny

class Base(TypedDict):
    foo: str


# Equivalent to subclassing Base, as TDs are structural types
class Sub(TypedDict):
    foo: str
    bar: int


class Model(BaseModel):
    x: SerializeAsAny[Base]

m = Model(x=Sub(foo="hello", bar=123))
print(m)
#> x={'foo': 'hello'}
```

Given the default of `extra='ignore'`, this is _probably_ correct? It does seem surprising though. Maybe the semantics of `extra` on `TypedDict` need re-thinking.
Indeed seems like we missed this when discussing with @DouweM.

issues.title: 2.12: `serialize_as_any` issues
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Pydantic has a serialization concept called *serialize as any*, which can be enabled globally during serialization (using the `serialize_as_any` parameter) or per field (using the [`SerializeAsAny`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/functional_serializers/#pydantic.functional_serializers.SerializeAsAny) annotation). When used, Pydantic treats the type annotation of the field as if it were `Any`, and tries to infer the value using its actual type (see the [documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/serialization/#serializing-with-duck-typing) for more details).

Up until now, the `serialize_as_any` flag and `SerializeAsAny` annotation were behaving quite differently, and they were [unified](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1478) in 2.12 (actually, the `serialize_as_any` flag behavior was aligned to match the `SerializeAsAny` annotation).

Unfortunately this also led to regressions where cases that previously serialized in 2.11 no longer serialize in 2.12, because dropping the type information forces `pydantic-core` to use type inference, and not all types have built-in inference in `pydantic-core`:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, IPvAnyAddress

class BugReproModel(BaseModel):
    ip_address: IPvAnyAddress

bug = BugReproModel(ip_address="192.168.1.1")

json_data = bug.model_dump_json(serialize_as_any=True)
# PydanticSerializationError: Unable to serialize unknown type: <class 'ipaddress.IPv4Address'>
```

**If you are using the `serialize_as_any` flag and encountering serialization errors in 2.12**:

1. Check if you can make use of the `SerializeAsAny` annotation instead, and apply it only on fields if this is necessary.
2. If you don't control the types you are serializing (e.g. a Pydantic model defined in a third party library), we'd like to know more about your `serialize_as_any` usage. If you use it to achieve [duck typing serialization](http
issues.author: davidhewitt

comments.body: Update: the [latest patch release](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/releases/tag/v2.12.4) includes a fix to support serialization of IP address types. Hopefully next week, we will ship a new patch release with a new *polymorphic serialization* behavior, that should probably be used instead of *serialize as any*.

Apologies for the really long delay on this one. As we migrated `pydantic-core` into pydantic, it took us some time to set up CI properly.

I just released [v2.13.0b1](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/releases/tag/v2.13.0b1), which provides the new `polymorphic_serialization` option and should solve the issues introduced in 2.12.

A final release will follow in one or two weeks.

In our organization we use Pydantic to serialize artifacts from experiments devised by scientists. In this context there is never a case where we would want to exclude fields from a model which are present on subclasses, but not on the base. That is, we want to duck-type everything - the more data we collect the better. The original behavior of `serialize_as_any` as of 2.11 did just this for us.

Absent the 2.11 behavior of implicit duck-typing, our scientists will need to defensively annotate every field containing a nested model with `SerializeAsAny` in case someone uses a subclass that has extra fields. Trying to explain this rather esoteric behavior to our scientist colleagues is something we'd like to avoid.

Is there a way to recover the 2.11 `serialize_as_any` behavior in any way?

I'm happy to provide more details about our use case if the description above is too vague.

> we may introduce a new value for serialize_as_any(e.g. a literal 'subclasses', name TBD), on top of the existing True/False.

If I understand correctly. This may be what I'm looking for. Is there a way to follow progress on a `serialize_as_any="subclass"` feature?
Could you explain why https://docs.pydantic.dev/dev/concepts/serialization/#polymorphic-serialization doesn't suit your needs?

Ah, was not aware of the feature. I suspect that'll suit my needs. I came here after reading [this blog post](https://pydantic.dev/articles/pydantic-v2-12-release#unify-serialize_as_anyserializeasany-behavior-section) which doesn't seem to mention it.
Pydantic [v2.13.0](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/releases/tag/v2.13.0) was just released.

issues.title: `exclude_if` for extra fields not used
issues.body:

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

New `exclude_if` annotation in `Field` ignored on extras

Xref https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1835#discussion_r2425955815

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Annotated

IntExcludeZero = Annotated[int, Field(exclude_if=lambda v: v == 0)]

class Model(BaseModel):
    model_config = {
        'extra': 'allow'
    }
    __pydantic_extra__: dict[str, IntExcludeZero]

    a: IntExcludeZero

m = Model(a=0, b=0)
m.model_dump()  # {'b': 0}
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
2.12.0
```

Good question. I think given this would be specific to each model, it doesn't need to go in the `Extra` state, which is for globals.

Probably this needs to be passed in via the `model_fields_schema` and stored in the `GeneralFieldsSerializer`?
Something like that seems fine, yes. I think the hard part will be the Python code to send this information into the serialization machinery.

issues.title: Pydantic V2 and V2 core local change sync issue
issues.body:
Ran into it while working on #12321 , basically pydantic sets the version  of pydantic core to 'pydantic-core==2.41.5' in its dependencies in pyproject.toml.  So, new validation logic for Sequence tested for pydantic-core does not really reflect in pydantic. This is kinda weird given that they are in a monorepo despite being separate packages.

What's the recommended way to develop/test changes across both packages? Should I add a make install-local target for editable installs, or is there an existing workflow I should use?
issues.author: tanmaymunjal

comments.body: Using `uv`, `pydantic-core` should rebuild on every `uv run` invocation, if files changed in the `pydantic-core` directory.  @davidhewitt I think we should probably have a makefile target that builds and installs pydantic-core locally so that it can be run manually. This is especially useful as I personally don't use `uv run` for DX reasons, and also because it will trigger a sync of the venv, which will delete all manually installed dependencies in the venv (which is quite common for me as I need to test MREs with external dependencies).

`make install` should always do it, I can also set up some additional make targets specifically for building core.

@tanmaymunjal note that I have been making several improvements to the dev flow since we merged pydantic-core into this repository (only a few weeks ago) so it's possible this already works better than as you found it.
I do think I might have a fix internally while working on the Sequence issue. I still got to do a code cleanup on the pr though,will prob  check and send a pr fixing both. @davidhewitt

Hello! With this monorepo setup, what’s the recommended way to create PRs for `pydantic-core` while passing pre-commit checks? I’m working on issue #12387  and added changes to `pydantic-core` that cause `pyright` to fail on the `pydantic` side. When I run `make install`, the local version of `pydantic-core` is installed in my virtual environment, which forces me to update `pydantic` as well. Is this the new workflow? Should PRs now include changes to both `pydantic-core` and `pydantic` at the same time?

Yes, PRs can now modify both codebases at the same time. That was the main benefit of moving to the monorepo setup - the full implementation can be written in one shot, with tests, documentation etc in a single PR.

issues.title: Evolving Pydantic: `BaseStruct`
issues.body:
This is a feature plan for the next step in the `pydantic` API, which we'd like to call `BaseStruct`.

The high-level goal is to take some of the lessons we've learned from `BaseModel`, and present a new type which should achieve higher performance, more features and better default semantics. `BaseModel` may eventually gain some of these changes itself, but we would prefer to be extremely conservative with changing `BaseModel` and introduce a new type so that users can opt-in to the changed semantics.

We will merge `BaseStruct` into the `pydantic.experimental.structs` namespace while this type is being developed, possibly as soon as Pydantic 2.13. We anticipate that Pydantic V3 may be the milestone where `BaseStruct` is stabilised, although this is not strictly required.

Rough outline of the planned semantics of `BaseStruct`:

- Performance
   - [ ] `BaseStruct` is planned to keep more data as (internal?) native state rather than Python objects
   - [ ] Field accessors will probably be cached so that once the Python value is accessed, it will have normal Python semantics
- Ergonomics
   - [ ] `BaseStruct` will have no methods (`model_validate`, `model_dump`). Instead we will expose free functions such as `validate`, `validate_json`, `to_python` and `to_json`
   - [ ] There will likely be a `StructMethodsMixin` type to add methods such as `struct_validate` where desired
   - [ ] We might try to do something different with generic structs so that we don't create new subclasses when doing generic parameterisation, and just make "regular" generic aliases.
- Features
  - [ ] We would like to eventually offer compatibilty between struct types and dataframes, particularly arrow "struct arrays". The exact form of this is yet to be explored.
issues.author: davidhewitt

comments.body: I repurposed #10032 to be the issue for this work, will close this one.

issues.title: Empty dict serialization for dataclass subclasses in union types (Pydantic 2.12.4)
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When a Pydantic `BaseModel` field uses a union type (`BaseModel | JSONMixin`) and contains a nested `BaseModel` with a dataclass subclass instance, `model_dump()` produces an empty dict `{}` in Pydantic 2.12.4. This works correctly in Pydantic 2.12.3.

### Version Information

- **Pydantic version**: 2.12.4 (bug occurs) / 2.12.3 (works correctly)
- **pydantic-core version**: 2.41.5 (bug occurs) / 2.41.4 (works correctly)
- **Python version**: 3.12+
- **OS**: Any

### Example Code

```Python
import dataclasses
import fastclasses_json
import pydantic


@fastclasses_json.dataclass_json
@dataclasses.dataclass
class BaseError(fastclasses_json.JSONMixin):
    code: str
    message: str


@fastclasses_json.dataclass_json
@dataclasses.dataclass
class NetworkError(BaseError):
    code: str = dataclasses.field(default='network_error', init=False)


class Result(pydantic.BaseModel):
    error: BaseError


class Container(pydantic.BaseModel):
    model_config = pydantic.ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)  # Required for JSONMixin in union
    
    message: pydantic.BaseModel | fastclasses_json.JSONMixin


def main() -> None:
    print(f"Pydantic {pydantic.__version__}\n")

    base_error = BaseError(code='base', message='test')
    network_error = NetworkError(message='test')
    network_error_dict = NetworkError(message='test').to_dict()

    container_base = Container(message=Result(error=base_error))
    container_network = Container(message=Result(error=network_error))
    container_network_dict = Container(message=Result(error=network_error_dict))

    print(f"BaseError:        {'✓' if container_base.model_dump()['message'] != {} else '✗'}")
    print(f"NetworkError:     {'✓' if container_network.model_dump()['message'] != {} else '✗✗✗ BUG'}")
    print(f"Convert to dict:  {'✓' if container_network_dict.model_dump()['message'] != {} else '✗'}")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```
issues.author: kusaku

comments.body:
MRE:

```python
import dataclasses
import pydantic


class Base:
    pass


@dataclasses.dataclass
class BaseError(Base):
    code: str
    message: str


@dataclasses.dataclass
class NetworkError(BaseError):
    code: str = dataclasses.field(default='network_error', init=False)


class Result(pydantic.BaseModel):
    error: BaseError


class Container(pydantic.BaseModel):
    model_config = pydantic.ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)  # Required for JSONMixin in union
    message: pydantic.BaseModel | Base


print(f"Pydantic {pydantic.__version__}\n")

base_error = BaseError(code='base', message='test')
network_error = NetworkError(message='test')

container_base = Container(message=Result(error=base_error))
container_network = Container(message=Result(error=network_error))

print(container_network.model_dump())
#> {'message': {}}  in 2.12.4
```

cc @davidhewitt, might be from https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1855? The 2.12.3 behavior looks wrong, as it shouldn't apply this kind of "serialize as any" behavior here.

Interestingly, replacing

```python
message: pydantic.BaseModel | Base
```

with

```python
message: pydantic.BaseModel
```

gets the empty value in `2.12.3` also.

EDIT: of course it does, there is no "serialize as any" behavour applicable so `pydantic.BaseModel` as an annotation will serialize to nothing. @Viicos I wonder if we can make that less of a footgun.
`Base` is an arbitrary type, so we will always use type inference to serialize it.  The 2.12.3 behavior therefore seems correct.

> `Base` is an arbitrary type, so we will always use type inference to serialize it. The 2.12.3 behavior therefore seems correct.

But in this case `container_network.result` is a Pydantic model, so I don't see why it should serialize against `Base`? I would assume it should use the `pydantic.BaseModel` member of the union, and as such serialize to an empty dict because `serialize_as_any` doesn't apply here.

Yes, you're correct, sorry. I was just reaching similar conclusions.

The behavior change has come from https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1857/files#diff-6a585ccfa8f15256fde9fddfa8d1c651a69b9f20d4d320a916cbef5ab6d4ae48L97-L117, where we started passing the full extra from the current serialization into model serialization during type inference, rather than building a new `Extra` with `SerCheck::None`. That change means that type inference properly respects current union behavior, which I defend as correct.

The explanation here then, is that on 2.12.3 the `Base` member was serialized under type inference:
- the `Result` is a `BaseModel`, so we hit the case above
- which would drop the union branch checking as per the patch above not having been applied
- ... and so would serialize the contained `NetworkError` as a subtype of `BaseError`

On 2.12.4, we no longer drop type checking inside the union when doing type inference, so:

- when serializing the `Result` under type inference for the `Base` union member, we are still running with `SerCheck::Strict` in the union branch checking
- ... and so `Result` serialization fails because the contained `NetworkError` is not exactly a `BaseError`
- ... so we retry the whole union with to "Lax" branch checking, where `Result` _is_ a subtype of `BaseModel`
- in the first union member `Result` is a subtype of `BaseModel`, so in Lax type checking we will serialize this as a `BaseModel` (i.e empty dict, because `BaseModel` has no fields)

This is a case where #12518 probably helps (possibly as an annotation), where the right solution might be:

```python
class Container(pydantic.BaseModel):
    model_config = pydantic.ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)  # Required for Base in union
    message: PolymorphicSerialization[pydantic.BaseModel] | Base
```

... or of course, could set it in the model config which might be totally fine too.

With polymorphic serialization enabled, in the Lax branch checking the `Result` would serialize as a `Result`, and I _believe_ that the dataclass subclass would also be accepted, leading to the desired result.

> ... or of course, could set it in the model config which might be totally fine too.

I just attempted to verify this, it doesn't currently apply because `polymorphic_serialization` as implemented affects `Container`, not its fields. Might influence the design of #12518

... and trying the runtime value shows a bug on #12518, it breaks stdlib dataclasses.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/david/Dev/pydantic/pydantic/test.py", line 45, in <module>
    print(container_network.model_dump(polymorphic_serialization=True))
          ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/Users/david/Dev/pydantic/pydantic/pydantic/main.py", line 466, in model_dump
    return self.__pydantic_serializer__.to_python(
           ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^
        self,
        ^^^^^
    ...<13 lines>...
        polymorphic_serialization=polymorphic_serialization,
        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
    )
    ^
AttributeError: 'NetworkError' object has no attribute '__pydantic_serializer__'
```

... I will fix that on #12518 and circle back here.

With latest pushes to #12518, can confirm that branch would fix this case:

```python
print(container_network.model_dump(polymorphic_serialization=True))
#> {'message': {'error': {'code': 'network_error', 'message': 'test'}}}
```

issues.title: Exponential perfomance drop (100k%) for edge cases serialization in `pydantic>=2.11.0b1` (0.044 seconds -> 183.207 seconds)
issues.author: Danipulok

comments.body:
Not a 100% sure yet, but it may be that https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1652 introduced the issue. There is also something wrong (which seems to have been the case for a long time): every member of the union is tried during serialization, even though it is a discriminated one. This can be seen reducing the repro to:

```python
tools_adapter = TypeAdapter(Tool)  # instead of list[Tool]
tool = WrapTool(
    input_schema=Schema(
        **gen_json_schema(num_properties=1),  # instead of 65
    ),
    tool=InnerTool(),
)

tools_adapter = TypeAdapter(Tool)
print("serialization.start")
time_start = time.time()
data = tools_adapter.dump_python(tools[0])
time_end = time.time()
time_elapsed = time_end - time_start
print("serialization.end")
print("PYDANTIC VERSION", __version__)
print(f"Time taken: {time_elapsed} seconds")

"""
serialization.start
t5.py:50: UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `InnerTool` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `enum` but got `str` with value `'disabled'` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `ToolType1` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `ToolType2` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `ToolType3` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `ToolType4` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `ToolType5` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  return handler(self)
.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/pydantic/type_adapter.py:451: UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `InnerTool` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `enum` but got `str` with value `'disabled'` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `ToolType1` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `ToolType2` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `ToolType3` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `ToolType4` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue: Expected `ToolType5` but got `WrapTool` with value `WrapTool(type='tool:wrap'...r', my_enum='disabled'))` - serialized value may not be as expected
  Expected `enum` but got `str` with value `'disabled'` - serialized value may not be as expected
  return self.serializer.to_python
"""
```

Opened a fix in pydantic-core: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1902

Update: Ported the fix to pydantic directly: #12825

@navalprakhar unfortunately I don't have `rust` env setup ready. Is there any way you could please check my MRE and send the results?

The new script metadata:
```python
# /// script
# dependencies = [
#     "pydantic==2.12.5",
#     "pydantic-core @ git+https://github.com/navalprakhar/pydantic-core@fix/tagged-union-serialization-fallback",
# ]
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# ///
```

@Danipulok Ran your MRE against the fix in #12825 

**Before (main):** ~1.44s
**After (fix):** ~0.018s

## Output (with fix)

```
serialization.start
mre-12800.py:60: UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
    PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `enum` - ... [field_name='my_enum', input_value='disabled', input_type=str])
    return handler(self)
... (1 warning per tool, accumulating across 10 tools — not per union member)
serialization.end
PYDANTIC VERSION 2.13.0a0+dev
Time taken: 0.018275737762451172 seconds
```

The warnings are expected. They correctly flag the `str`/`enum` mismatch (`'disabled'` instead of `MyStrangeEnum.DISABLED`), but the serializer no longer falls back to trying all other union members when the discriminator already matched the correct variant.

@navalprakhar, wow, cool!
Seems really nice. Thank you very much for the fix!

> [@davidhewitt](https://github.com/davidhewitt) will look into this in more depth. From what we discussed, trying all discriminated union members was most likely done to preserve some compatibility with normal unions, but I don't think it really makes sense to do so given that in theory other union members shouldn't serialize as well due to the discriminator value being different.
> 
> If we change the serialization logic, we should be careful adapting it when we tackle [#11188](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/11188).
> 
> Apart from the serialization behavior, we should also look into why the wrap serializer affects performance, and if [pydantic/pydantic-core#1652](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1652) is relevant.

Makes sense. Just to clarify: #12825 only adjusts the branch where the discriminator already matched. The no match/fallback behavior that #11188 would need is unchanged.

@Viicos, hey!
Is there a way for the fix PR to get merged?
As @navalprakhar stated, it doesn't change the behavior for #11188
And I think the changed in the PR should be merged anyway, since it's a high perfomance optimization

We'll want to merge https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12825 for the 2.13 release, but it is important for us to understand why it regressed.

TL;DR: Serialization warnings were raised more than necessary, and this became noticeable since we started lazily computing the string repr of warnings.

Here is my investigation:

In https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1652, the serialization errors (that can be raised as warnings or exceptions) were changed to be lazily computed (to improve performance, as the `repr()` of the values are now not computed if unnecessary -- i.e. if warnings are ignored).

However, it also means the string representation of such errors is computed every time it is needed. This happens in `CollectWarnings.final_check()`:

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/1a37a3d479dea9b21c7379e882bf5ec56fb94fc2/pydantic-core/src/serializers/extra.rs#L508-L525

Prior to https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1652, `CollectWarnings` would just contain a `Vec` of strings, the error string was computed _when_ added to the `Vec`.

`final_check()` is being called from several places:

- in `SchemaSerializer.to_[python/json]()`:

    https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/1a37a3d479dea9b21c7379e882bf5ec56fb94fc2/pydantic-core/src/serializers/mod.rs#L90-L133

    This is where the unique `CollectWarnings` instance is created (as part of the `state`), and `final_check()` is called after serialization is completed.

- in `SerializationCallable.__call__()` (also from `SerializationIterator` but we'll not focus on this one):

    https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/1a37a3d479dea9b21c7379e882bf5ec56fb94fc2/pydantic-core/src/serializers/type_serializers/function.rs#L462-L495

    `SerializationCallable` is the `handler` instance being passed to wrap serializers. Whenever this handler is called, it delegates to the inner serializer, calls `final_check()`, and returns the value back to the Python serializer function.


The thing is, in the provided example, the wrap serializer ends up being called 1155 times (expected given the deeply nested structure of the data), which results in 1155 calls to `final_check()` (out of which 1002 ends up raising a warning as the warnings `Vec` isn't empty -- note that not all of them are written to `stderr`, there are a lot of duplicates [that are being suppressed](https://docs.python.org/3/library/warnings.html#:~:text=Repetitions%20of%20a%20particular%20warning%20for%20the%20same%20source%20location%20are%20typically%20suppressed)).

During the serialization process, a _unique_ `CollectWarnings` instance is used, so presumably these `final_check()` calls in the handler aren't necessary, and will only lead to duplicates (as the `final_check()` call in `SchemaSerializer.to_[python/json]()` will emit the warnings anyway).

If we serialize with `warnings='error'`, removing this `final_check()` call might change the behavior, because the error would previously be raised when calling the handler, which wouldn't be the case now. However, such errors would still be raised in the `final_check()` call in `SchemaSerializer.to_[python/json]()`, so it would be fine?

Having errors raised within the handler also results in confusing results:

```python
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import WrapSerializer

class Model(BaseModel):
    a: tuple[int, int]
    b: Annotated[int, WrapSerializer(lambda v, h: h(v))] | str


m = Model(a=(1, 2), b=1)

# Change the tuple so that a warning is stored in `CollectWarnings`.
# The tuple serialization logic is one of the couple places where serialization
# can be retried in "lax" mode.
m.a = (1, 2, 3)

m.model_dump()
"""
UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Unexpected extra items present in tuple)
"""
# We get the warning, as expected.

m.model_dump(warnings='error')
"""
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.PydanticSerializationError: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Unexpected extra items present in tuple)
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(PydanticSerializationError: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Unexpected extra items present in tuple))
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `str` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='b', input_value=1, input_type=int])
"""

# What happened here:
# - Field `a` is serialized first. It produced a warning which was stored in `CollectWarnings`.
# - Field `b` is serialized.
#    - The first member of the union is attempted. the serializer handler is called,
#      serializes using the inner serializer (for the `int` type) successfully, and calls `final_check()`.
#      Because we had a warning present from field `a`, it is raised as an error and assumed to be
#      an error during the inner serialization.
#    - The union serializer tries serializing against the second member of the union (`str`) which
#      fails because of the type mismatch -- but we shouldn't try this union member to begin with!
```

The test suite fully passes with the `final_check()` call removed in the handler. I've tried looking for ways this could introduce a regression (e.g. a union serialization relying on a warning being raised as an error), and theoretically it can only alter the warnings/error output.

@Viicos thank you very much for the investigation! Waiting for this to get merged and solved.

I read your comment and partially understood what happened. But I just want to remind you that there was double regression in benchmarks.

```
2.12.5 - 5s
2.12.0b1 - 5s
2.11.10 - 5s
2.11.5 - 5s
2.11.3 - 5s
2.11.2 - 5s
2.11.1 - 3.2s
2.11.0 - 3.2s
2.11.0b2 - 3.4s
2.11.0b1 - 3.3s
2.11.0a2 - 0.025s
2.11.0a1 -  0.024s
2.10.6 - 0.03s
```

1. Initial regression happened between `2.11.0a2` and `2.11.0b1`.
2. Second regression happened between `2.11.1` and `2.11.2`.

Just reminding you this, since from my understanding, you were looking only at one of the related commits

> During the serialization process, a unique CollectWarnings instance is used, so presumably these final_check() calls in the handler aren't necessary, and will only lead to duplicates (as the final_check() call in SchemaSerializer.to_[python/json]() will emit the warnings anyway).

This isn't exactly true, checking `ExtraOwned::to_state` it looks like the `CollectWarnings` instance is cloned when calling the wrap handler. That looks potentially expensive and likely wrong, because warnings inside the wrap serialization won't propagate upwards but warnings from above are propagating down.

If serialization fails inside the wrap handler, what's the expected UX? I guess we do expect the handler to raise the exception, which makes me think the `final_check()` _is_ necessary. But I think probably there should be an empty `CollectWarnings` created when calling the handler, rather than one propagated down from above?

> If serialization fails inside the wrap handler, what's the expected UX? I guess we do expect the handler to raise the exception, which makes me think the `final_check()` _is_ necessary. But I think probably there should be an empty `CollectWarnings` created when calling the handler, rather than one propagated down from above?

Why would calling a wrap serializer handler result in some kind of "checkpoint" on warnings? I understand it would make sense to do something like this:

```python
def ser(v, h):
    try:
        return h(v)
    except Exception:
        return ...  # return a fallback value, or retry the handler
```

But I don't think this behaves as we expect, as currently an exception will be raised if:

- `warnings='error'` is set on the serialization method.
- the wrap serializer is used within a union, in which case `SerCheck::Strict` is used which results in errors.

Both of these options aren't controlled by the wrap serializer itself, so it feels like a fragile pattern to use. For instance, consider the following example:

```python
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import TypeAdapter, WrapSerializer

def ser(v, h):
    try:
        return h(v)
    except Exception:
        return "fallback"

ta1 = TypeAdapter(Annotated[str, WrapSerializer(ser)])
ta1.dump_python(1)
"""
UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `str` - serialized value may not be as expected [input_value=1, input_type=int])
"""
#> 1

# Now with a union:

ta2 = TypeAdapter(Annotated[str, WrapSerializer(ser)] | int)
ta2.dump_python()
#> "fallback"
```

In any case, it seems like we can't remove the `final_check()` call because some users might be using this `try..except` pattern inside wrap serializers.

> But I think probably there should be an empty `CollectWarnings` created when calling the handler, rather than one propagated down from above?

That would make sense, and I'm wondering if the collected warnings within the serializer should also be propagated to the caller?

> That would make sense, and I'm wondering if the collected warnings within the serializer should also be propagated to the caller?

I think these are already "propagated" by the exception as you demonstrated above, but the conversion of them is just pretty bad. We should probably just make sure that the conversion of warnings -> exception -> warnings is as low cost as possible (maybe delay formatting until actually rendered), maybe by storing an internal datastructure of the warnings inside the exception which we can pull out cheaply when adding them to the caller's warnings.

> I think these are already "propagated" by the exception as you demonstrated above

This is when they appear as exceptions, which is only the case in the two bullet points I mentioned. In the general case, I feel like the warnings should be propagated to the outer caller, because right now it acts weirdly:

```python
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel, WrapSerializer


class Model(BaseModel):
    a: Annotated[str, WrapSerializer(lambda v, h: h(v))]
    b: str
    c: str

m = Model(a="t", b="t", c="t")
m.a = 1
m.b = 2
m.c = 3

m.model_dump()
# Two separate warnings:
"""
UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `str` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='a', input_value=1, input_type=int])
UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `str` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='b', input_value=2, input_type=int])
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `str` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='c', input_value=3, input_type=int])
"""
```

Maybe we could special case the `final_check()` call within the handler to raise an error if the warnings mode is set to `'error'`, and otherwise just propagate the collected warnings?

To follow up on the initial regression, if https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12995 isn't tackled and we get similar reports still, we could make sure we cache the result of `PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue.__repr__()` in `final_check()`.

issues.title: Spurious Serialization Warnings with List of Discriminated Union
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I'm seeing unexpected warnings when dumping a **validated** model using `model_dump` and `model_dump_json`. This occurs on a model with a field that is a `list[SomeDiscriminatedUnion]`. See the minimal example below. It is odd that these warnings occur only during serialization, but never during deserialization. 

Presumably this warning serves to warn of a developer error in defining the models or some kind of issue with the model content. Based on the second case below, it doesn't not seem to be an intrinsic issue with the models. However, in all cases, the content is validated. So I would expect all of the code blocks below to serialize without a warning.

Seems like a good candidate for this milestone https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/milestone/13 

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Annotated, Literal, Union

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class ModelA(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["A"] = "A"
    value_a: str


class ModelB(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["B"] = "B"
    value_b: int


model_classes = {ModelA, ModelB}

ModelUnion = Annotated[Union[tuple(model_classes)], Field(discriminator="type")]


class ContainerModel(BaseModel):
    model: list[ModelUnion] = Field(default_factory=list)


def validate_and_serialize(container: ContainerModel):
    validated = ContainerModel.model_validate(container)
    return validated.model_dump_json()


# This emits a warning (thus the issue)
container_instance = ContainerModel(model=[])
container_instance.model.append({"type": "A", "value_a": "Hello"})
validate_and_serialize(container_instance)

# This does not emit a warning, so editing the model isn't the problem
container_instance = ContainerModel(model=[])
a_instance = ModelA(value_a="Hello")  # This does not incur a warning
container_instance.model.append(a_instance)

# This does not emit a warning either. Perhaps there is some difference in validation of ContainerMo
issues.author: rtumin2

comments.body:
PR #12825 (targeted at #12800) should also help here.

It stops the tagged union serializer from trying all union members when the discriminator already matched, which eliminates the extra spurious warnings.

These warnings seem reasonable to me: `{'type': 'A', 'value_a': 'Hello'}` is a `dict`, not a `ModelA`.

As your second example illustrates, if you changed the call to instead append a proper `ModelA` instance, then the serialization does not warn.

Possibly the surprising behavior here is actually that in your `validate_and_serialize` function, the `ContainerModel` instance is not actually validated, because it's an existing instance, so the `dict` is not corrected to be a `ModelA` instance. See https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.revalidate_instances

If you set the `revalidate_instances=True` config parameter on `ContainerModel`, there are no more warnings.

issues.title: Releasing GIL during model_validate / model_dump
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Hello - thanks for the great project! 

Has there been any consideration for releasing the GIL during Rust code execution? It seems like many users could benefit from performance speedups in threaded applications if that were implemented. Is it feasible to do this safely in general? If not, perhaps it could be opt-in for a safe subset of models?

To be clear I am not referring to nogil operation - though the features may be related. 

I have profiled my application and do currently spend a fair bit of time on validation / serialization.

If contributions are welcome along this line that would also be great to know. Thank you! 

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [x] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [x] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [ ] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [x] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.
issues.author: mharradon

comments.body: The Rust code performs a lot of Python operations, I suspect it's not really helpful to release the GIL during validation or serialiation as it'll just be repeatedly re-acquired for the Python ops.
Closing as not planned as per the above.
That makes sense - thanks!

> The Rust code performs a lot of Python operations, I suspect it's not really helpful to release the GIL during validation or serialiation as it'll just be repeatedly re-acquired for the Python ops.

But at least it would let a chance to run other threads? I'm particularly interested in FastAPI running validation/serialization in a thread in async handlers (using e.g. `asyncio.to_thread`).

Another idea, if we want to stay in a mono-threaded environment, is to provide async versions of the validation/serialization functions, that would just checkpoint to the event-loop often enough so that it's not blocked for too long (that still needs releasing the GIL before checkpointing).

I suppose it's possible that we could have some kind of interval after which we'd release / reacquire the GIL to allow other threads to potentially make progress. We could also explore whether there are particularly heavy operations which would make sense to release the GIL for. My general impression is that most Rust-only ops are generally very fine-grain so releasing the GIL is likely to be significant overhead.

If you have a specific example where Pydantic is clearly not cooperating with other threads, that'd be helpful to investigate.

FWIW my understanding is that if we enter Python code from within Rust code (which is very possible in many points, e.g. to call model validators, plain serializers, even class `__init__`) then the interpreter's usual eval breaker mechanism can kick in and allow thread switching. I haven't actually confirmed that, though.

> If you have a specific example where Pydantic is clearly not cooperating with other threads, that'd be helpful to investigate.

This is an example using FastAPI and a big pydantic model that is used to serialize data:
```py
from time import monotonic
from typing import Annotated

import httpx
import uvicorn
from anyio import create_task_group, run, sleep
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel, create_model


fields = {
    f"key{i}": (
        Annotated[str, "something"],
        f"val{i}",
    ) for i in range(100_000)
}

Item = create_model(
    "Item",
    **fields,
)


async def make_request():
    await sleep(1)
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        await client.get("http://127.0.0.1:8000/item/")


async def mesure_blocking_time():
    blocking_time = 0
    while True:
        t0 = monotonic()
        await sleep(0.01)
        t1 = monotonic()
        if (_blocking_time := t1 - t0) > blocking_time:
            blocking_time = _blocking_time
            print(f"{blocking_time=}")


async def main():
    app = FastAPI()

    @app.get("/item/")
    async def get_item():
        return Item()

    async with create_task_group() as tg:
        tg.start_soon(make_request)
        tg.start_soon(mesure_blocking_time)
        config = uvicorn.Config(app)
        server = uvicorn.Server(config)
        await server.serve()


run(main)
```
If you run it you'll see something like that:
```
INFO:     Started server process [178556]
INFO:     Waiting for application startup.
INFO:     Application startup complete.
INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000 (Press CTRL+C to quit)
blocking_time=0.011099938012193888
blocking_time=0.011408488993765786
blocking_time=0.04857334599364549
INFO:     127.0.0.1:39636 - "GET /item/ HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
blocking_time=0.5352073440153617
```
That is, the event-loop max blocking time is low until the request it made, at which point the event loop is blocked for ~0.5 seconds.

In the above example everything runs in the main thread, the model creation and its serialization. The latter is done by FastAPI, but this shows that the model creation already is not cooperative:
```py
import anyio

async def main():
    async with create_task_group() as tg:
        tg.start_soon(mesure_blocking_time)
        await sleep(1)
        await anyio.to_thread.run_sync(Item)
```

issues.title: Support `memoryview` in `model_validate_json`
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

[model_validate_json](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/base_model/#pydantic.BaseModel.model_validate_json) currently only accepts `str | bytes | bytearray` as the type of the input JSON data. For zero-copy-ish workloads it would be great to also support `memoryview`, so one doesn't need to copy the JSON data just to have it validated.

For reference, this is the error one currently gets when trying to pass a `memoryview` to `model_validate_json`:

```
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for X
  JSON input should be string, bytes or bytearray [type=json_type, input_value=<memory at 0x110d693c0>, input_type=memoryview]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.10/v/json_type
```

- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)

issues.title: model_dump(exclude_unset=True) produces spurious warnings for Union members with unset defaults
issues.body:
## Description

`model_dump(exclude_unset=True)` on a model containing a `Union` field produces spurious `PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue` warnings when a union member has unset fields with defaults.

## Reproduction

```python
from typing import Literal

from pydantic import BaseModel


class Cat(BaseModel):
    type: Literal['cat']
    color: str | None = None  # field with default


class Dog(BaseModel):
    type: Literal['dog']


class Zoo(BaseModel):
    animals: list[Cat | Dog]


cat = Cat(type='cat')
zoo = Zoo(animals=[cat])

# Sanity check: model_dump without exclude_unset works fine
assert zoo.model_dump() == {'animals': [{'type': 'cat', 'color': None}]}

# Sanity check: model_dump(exclude_unset=True) on the Cat alone works fine
assert cat.model_dump(exclude_unset=True) == {'type': 'cat'}

# BUG: model_dump(exclude_unset=True) on the parent Zoo produces warnings
assert zoo.model_dump(exclude_unset=True) == {'animals': [{'type': 'cat'}]}
```

The last line produces:

```
UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected 2 fields but got 1: Expected `Cat` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='animals', ...])
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `Dog` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='animals', ...])
```

The output is correct, but the warnings are spurious.

## Version info

- **Broken**: pydantic 2.13.0b2 / pydantic-core 2.42.0 (installed from latest on github)
- **Works**: pydantic 2.12.5 / pydantic-core 2.41.5
- Python 3.12
issues.author: alexmojaki

comments.body: Thanks, bisected to https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12677.

comments.body:
Following up on the discussion from #12800. I agree with the direction of fresh `CollectWarnings` in the handler + propagating back to the parent + keeping `final_check()` only for error mode.

One thing I was thinking about is how to actually get the warnings back to the parent, since the `SerializationCallable` gets moved into Python when passed to `call1`.

We could use `Py::new() + clone_ref()` to keep a handle on the Rust side, then `borrow_mut` after the Python function returns to drain warnings back into the parent state.

Lmk what you think, happy to take a stab at this.

issues.title: `__pydantic_extra__` ignored if `extra = 'forbid'` at model level, overridden at runtime
issues.body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

After #12233 it is possible to pass `extra='allow'` at runtime to temporarily override `extra` setting. However, if this is done, the `__pydantic_extra__` type hint on the model (if present) is ignored.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel


class A(BaseModel, extra='forbid'):
    __pydantic_extra__: dict[str, int]
    a: int


# Passes validation even though 'test' is not an int
a = A.model_validate({'a': 1, 'b': 'test'}, extra='allow')
print(a)  # A(a=1, b='test')


class B(A, extra='allow'):
    pass


# Fails validation, 'test' is not an int
b = B.model_validate({'a': 1, 'b': 'test'})
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.13.0b3
        pydantic-core version: 2.44.0
          pydantic-core build: profile=debug pgo=false
               python version: 3.14.3 (main, Feb  3 2026, 15:32:20) [Clang 17.0.0 (clang-1700.6.3.2)]
                     platform: macOS-26.4-arm64-arm-64bit-Mach-O
             related packages: mypy-1.18.2 typing_extensions-4.14.1 pyright-1.1.405 pydantic-extra-types-2.10.6 pydantic-settings-2.7.1
                       commit: 3468ec5d5
```
issues.author: davidhewitt

comments.body: Hi @davidhewitt I would like to work on it

This makes me wonder if it would have made more sense for the extra config to be something like `Literal['forbid', 'ignore'] | TypeForm[Any]`, so that you could also override the validated type at runtime (cross ref https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10758#issuecomment-2466161227).

### Discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/9565
Originally posted by **ZiChuanBuXiu** May 23, 2023 ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title here. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python NA ``` ### Description ``` from pydantic.typing import evaluate_forwardref, get_args, get_origin ImportError: cannot import name 'get_args' from 'pydantic.typing' (/Users/yfeng/Library/Caches/pypoetry/virtualenvs/aips-cq-resource-api-F3u0rVOe-py3.8/lib/python3.8/site-packages/pydantic/typing.cpython-38-darwin.so) ``` In `fastapi=0.95.2`, the dependency on `pydantic` is [here](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/0.95.2/pyproject.toml#L45) which indicates `pydantic=1.6.2` can be used. issues.author: Kludex

comments.body: Given how obvious the block is in code, should we assume that it was intentional and update the docs?

Hi, any update on this issue?

There is a similar issue to this one aswell

## Given a response defined in the api parameters

### This code produces a `Error: response status is 502`
```python
@router.get("/test")
def test(res: Response):
    # res.status_code = 200
    return res
```

>  last error log

```bash
File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages/uvicorn/protocols/http/h11_impl.py", line 488, in send
reason = STATUS_PHRASES[status]
```

### However if we change anything in the response

    return res
```

This is very weird because the default value of the `Response.status_code` is `200` anyway.
I was not able to understand how exactly can a `Response`  be put in the endpoint parameter list without `Depends` as it will not be explicitly initialized.

The couple of errors presented by @Kludex  & the one I reported above means maybe we need a big refactor on how Response class works? It has been over a year on this issue; I wonder why it has been up all this time ^^. Thank you

issues.title: HTTPBearer security scheme is returning 403 instead or 401
issues.body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/9130
Originally posted by **aaaaahaaaaa** September 8, 2020 HTTPBearer security scheme enabled as a dependency is returning a `403` when a request is unauthenticated because of a missing or a malformed `authorization` header. In those scenarios, a `401` should be returned instead.
## Related PRs - https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/2120 - https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5310 - https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5332 - https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/9312
issues.author: Kludex

comments.body: I use the `auto_error=False` and control the correct http error code manually.

Hey @Kludex, what do you think about raising a `HTTP 400 BAD REQUEST` for cases where the authentication scheme is not correctly 'bearer'?

[From RFC 6750 Section 3.1](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6750#section-3.1):

```
invalid_request
         The request is missing a required parameter, includes an
         unsupported parameter or parameter value, repeats the same
         parameter, uses more than one method for including an access
         token, or is otherwise malformed.  The resource server SHOULD
         respond with the HTTP 400 (Bad Request) status code.

invalid_token
         The access token provided is expired, revoked, malformed, or
         invalid for other reasons.  The resource SHOULD respond with
         the HTTP 401 (Unauthorized) status code.  The client MAY
         request a new access token and retry the protected resource
         request.
```

My reading of this text is that:

- when the server understands the request (i.e. 'bearer' scheme is correct) but the token itself is malformed we should return a `401`
- when the server does **not** understand the request (i.e. 'bearer' scheme is wrong), then we should return a `400`
Can you show me the diff you are talking about?

[`fastapi/security/http.py` L#125](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/security/http.py#L125-L130)

EDIT: Wrong line sorry
You want 400 instead of 403? That's why I asked the diff, not the lines in the code source. 👀

Sorry, my misunderstanding. At any rate, I don't think it is necessary after reading section 3 again I realised they offered an example for this scenario.

**tldr; 401 seems like the appropriate error code 👍** 

```
 If the request lacks any authentication information (e.g., the client
   was unaware that authentication is necessary or attempted using an
   unsupported authentication method), the resource server SHOULD NOT
   include an error code or other error information.

   For example:

     HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized
     WWW-Authenticate: Bearer realm="example"
```

This not only affects the `HTTPBearer`, but a lot of other classes in the `security` scope also return 403 in `"Not authenticated"` cases where they should return 401 instead, such as the `OAuth2` class (though strangely enough not the subclasses `OAuth2PasswordBearer` and `OAuth2AuthorizationCodeBearer`), the `HTTPBase` class and its sublasses (except for `HTTPBasic` for some reason), all classes in `api_key.py` and `OpenIdConnect`. Would probably be good if that could be straightened out for all occurrences there.

Will there be any progress in the future? Will this error be fixed? I just hope that when the client receives a 403 response, it prompts the user that they do not have access to the corresponding resource, and when a 401 response is received, it redirects to the login page. It seems that this cannot be achieved with the current default implementation of FastAPI.
For those who are interested in getting this issue resolved, please review #13786
This was handled in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/13786, available in FastAPI 0.122.0 🎉
This change broke some of our tests. As a workaround limit the version to `fastapi[all]<0.122.0"` or fix your tests to expect now 401 instead of 403.

> This change broke some of our tests. As a workaround limit the version to `fastapi[all]<0.122.0"` or fix your tests to expect now 401 instead of 403.

Consider also https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/how-to/authentication-error-status-code/

issues.title: Mounting sub-applications under `APIRouter`
issues.body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/8682

<div type='discussions-op-text'>

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter
from starlette.testclient import TestClient

app = FastAPI()
api_router = APIRouter(prefix="/api")


@api_router.get("/app")
def read_main():
    return {"message": "Hello World from main app"}


app.include_router(api_router)

subapi = FastAPI()


@subapi.get("/sub")
def read_sub():
    return {"message": "Hello World from sub API"}


api_router.mount("/subapi", subapi)

client = TestClient(app)

assert client.get('/api/app').status_code == 200

# this next assert fails
assert client.get('/api/subapi/sub').status_code == 200
```


### Description

Is it possible to mount a sub-application under an APIRouter? APIRouter itself has a mount function and accepts similar arguments to mounting a sub-application on a FastAPI instance, but I can't get the routing to actually work (nor can i get the openapi docs or spec to come back from that I would assume are the correct URLs.

The docs for sub applications note that the sub-application will have it's root_path correctly set, and I've tried a few combinations of manually setting the root_path on the subapi instance, but to no avail.

## Related
- https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/9070
- https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5373

Long story short, I couldn't make it work that way, so I just mounted v1 app to `myapp/api/v1` and v2 to `myapp/api/v2` directly to the "root" app, no routers between.

It is not obvious that the method originates from `starlette`'s base class and has no effect when used with `fastapi`. 

Not exactly sure why this is open for more than a year, but if there is no desire to close this functionality gap, it should at least raise an exception on misuse.

Out of curiosity is this still an issue? Sorry, I'm curious as to why the mounting of sub-apps to `APIRouter`?

The [documentation on sub-applications](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/sub-applications/) mounts the subapi FastiAPI app to right sub-domain by mounting the subapi app to the main app instance, i.e., `app.mount("/subapi", subapi)` which is what @jtougas' code does.

i found myself on this issue today. Find out that Mount object are indeed not added in the routes main object.
my usecase would be to add an authentication (through security dependency) by mounting a third party ASGI app, but I guess even if I can include the Mount in app routes, middlwares/dependencies will not be taken into account ?

Middleware and dependencies will not be triggered by the current Mount mecanism if I under the code correctly.
As a workaround, I used Mount with a custom Starlette middlware, but you loose the dependencies injection system that FastAPI provide.

Ran into this issue today:

```
from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter

hello_router: APIRouter = APIRouter()

@hello_router.get("/hello")
def hello_world():
    return {"message": "Hello, World!"}


greetings_router: APIRouter = APIRouter()

@greetings_router.get("/greetings")
def greetings():
    return {"message": "Greetings!"}


def app_factory() -> FastAPI:

    app: FastAPI = FastAPI()

    v1_api_router: APIRouter = APIRouter(prefix="/v1")
    v1_api_router.include_router(hello_router)

    sub_app: FastAPI = FastAPI()

    sub_app.include_router(greetings_router)
    v1_api_router.mount("/sub", sub_app)

    app.include_router(v1_api_router, prefix="/api")

    return app
```

Can't seem to access the route /api/v1/sub/greetings to get a response.

I wanted to use custom middleware for the set of routes in `sub_app`, seems like i'll have to use a hack to get this to work

referred: [discussion](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/9070)

this is a known limitation. While APIRouter inherits the .mount() method from Starlette, FastAPI's include_router logic is currently optimized to process APIRoute objects (standard endpoints). It doesn't correctly propagate Mount objects (sub-apps) when a router is included in a main app.

Currently, the best practice is to mount your sub-application directly to the main FastAPI instance using the full path prefix: app.mount("/api/v1/sub", sub_app). This ensures that the routing table and the root_path (for Swagger UI) are handled correctly.

Hi! I've opened PR #15246 which fixes this issue. It adds two changes to `fastapi/routing.py`:

1. An `APIRouter.mount()` override that prepends `self.prefix` to the path (matching how `add_api_route` works)
2. A `routing.Mount` branch in `include_router()` so mounted sub-apps are no longer silently dropped

All CI checks are green. Could a maintainer please add the `bug` label so the label check passes? Thanks!

```python
### Direct mounting of routes
import pathlib

from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter
import uvicorn
import pydantic

class HelloRequest(pydantic.BaseModel):
    id: str


class HelloResponse(pydantic.BaseModel):
    hello: str


class Hello:
    def __init__(self, name: str) -> None:
        self.name = name

    async def handle(self, request: HelloRequest) -> HelloResponse:
        return HelloResponse(hello=self.name)

app = FastAPI()
hello_handler = Hello(name="test")
app.add_api_route("/hello", hello_handler.handle, methods=["POST"])






### Mounting of paths via Router
import pathlib

from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter
import uvicorn
import pydantic

class HelloRequest(pydantic.BaseModel):
    id: str


class HelloResponse(pydantic.BaseModel):
    hello: str


class Hello:

        self.name = name

    async def handle(self, request: HelloRequest)

This is a valid issue. The type hint of both `endpoint` parameters should match.

Should be easy to fix - I don't know which one @tiangolo wants.

Hey @Kludex 

I want to help on this issue if no one is interested, I have used FastAPI before in some hobby projects but this is my first  OSS contribution!

I'll take this if no one else is!

> I don't know which one tiangolo wants.

I would think `Callable[..., Any]` is preferred:

- `FastAPI.add_api_route` makes a direct call to `APIRouter.add_api_route` anyways 
- `fastapi.dependencies.utils.get_typed_return_annotation` has signature `def get_typed_return_annotation(call: Callable[..., Any]) -> Any:`
- A lot of other functions with similar parameters use the same typing (e.g. `FastAPI.add_api_websocket_route`)
- The api router instantiates `route_class: Type[APIRoute]` of which `fastapi.routing.APIRoute` subclasses [`starlette.routing.Route` which also types `endpoint` with `Callable[..., typing.Any]`](https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/master/starlette/routing.py#L211-L215)

Happy to work with either

<div type='discussions-op-text'>

<sup>Originally posted by **DSamuylov** August 17, 2023</sup>

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
import bson
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import (
    field_validator,
    PlainSerializer,
    WithJsonSchema,
    BeforeValidator,
    TypeAdapter,
)
from fastapi import FastAPI


ObjectId = Annotated[
    bson.ObjectId,
    BeforeValidator(lambda x: bson.ObjectId(x) if isinstance(x, str) else x),
    PlainSerializer(lambda x: f"{x}", return_type=str),
    WithJsonSchema({"type": "string"}, mode="validation"),
    WithJsonSchema({"type": "string"}, mode="serialization"),
]

# Casting from str to ObjectId - WORKS
ta = TypeAdapter(
    ObjectId,
    config=dict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True),
)
ta.validate_python("5f7f9f0c2a0e0b0001d5b3d0")
ta.json_schema()

# Use ObjectId as a query parameter in FastAPI - FAILS:
app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/test")
def test(id: ObjectId) -> bool:
    return True
```


### Description

I am trying to figure out how to update my FastAPI app after from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2.
