`return StreamingCSVResponse(csv_stream, headers=headers, background=BackgroundTask(session.close))`
it worked for me but I'm not sure it's the correct way to go

Is there any update on this @tiangolo @Kludex ? I'm trying to figure out if we need to rewrite our `Depends` resource acquisition functions to be compatible with StreamingResponses.

It would be good to know if this was the intended behavior with the change in 0.106 or if this was an unintended side effect that may one day be fixed, even if a fix is not ready.

In my opinion, the change in 0.106 makes the dependency injection functionality of FastAPI a lot less powerful if it cannot be used with streaming responses.

> It would be good to know if this was the intended behavior with the change in 0.106 or if this was an unintended side effect that may one day be fixed, even if a fix is not ready.

The release note states that it as an intentional breaking change https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/release-notes/#01060

> Is there any update on this @tiangolo @Kludex ? I'm trying to figure out if we need to rewrite our `Depends` resource acquisition functions to be compatible with StreamingResponses.

I've pinged Sebastián. That's what I can do.

Thanks @falkben! And thanks @Kludex for the ping and help. :bow: 

Indeed, agreed that this is an unintended bug. And streaming responses are important for me/FastAPI, in fact I want to improve internal support for them, I have to experiment with that.

I haven't sat down to figure out the right way to solve this yet, but yes, it's under my radar.

The original intention with having dependencies close after the request is done was to allow using them in background tasks, thinking they could use the same database session. But later I realized that the work in a background task could end up being done much later, and keeping the same session would mean holding a DB connection that was not really needed for a long time (waiting for the network when the response is sent, so that's why I made dependencies close before sending the response. But I didn't realize that streaming responses would return something from the function, but would need the resource (DB session/connection) available until the response is finished sending, that was an oversight from my part.

I'm not sure what's the right way to fix it, I've considered a few approaches: having some attribute in responses marking them as finished in some way (the data is already there, they don't need any extra resource), so a streaming response would not be finished. Another alternative would be holding dependencies available and open when the return is a `Response` object, and only close them early if the response is an arbitrary object that FastAPI would automatically serialize.

Thanks very much! Could an extra attribute in the Depends call indicate whether to keep it around?

I also like the idea of keeping the dependency available if the return is a Response object, and hopefully wouldn't add much overhead.

I think it would still be beneficial to be able to use the dependencies in the background tasks.
* To reduce computation for creating and closings decencies.
* To avoid duplicate code for creating more complex dependencies.
* The dependency might contain state from the request handler that can be used in the background task.

This doesn't necessarily mean that all dependencies need to kept open until after the request is done. If there was a way to declare which dependencies the background task needs, the other dependencies could be closed early.

This also breaks things like this without `Depends`:
```python
@app.route("/get-file")
async def get_file():
    async with get_some_file_stream() as some_async_iterable:
        return StreamingResponse(
            content=some_async_iterable,
            headers=...,
            media_type=...,
        )
```

as the context is exited before finishing response streaming. I think this is not the expected behavior.

@schneebuzz this seems expected to me. Exiting the context manager closes the context. StreamResponse finishes only after it's returned.

If depends only closed after the response has completed, you could use them to turn the context into a procedure that returns the iterable and is cleaned up after the request has completed.

Hi!
I noticed the same problem (version 0.115.4), and I was confused for a moment because the documentation says the contrary: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/middleware/?h=middleware

> If you have dependencies with yield, the exit code will run after the middleware.

**You should at least modify this wrong documentation, and give more information about this until the final specification has been chosen and implemented to avoid to mislead and confuse the developers.**

In my case, I would like to commit the DB session only when it is certain that the 2xx status response will be sent without any problem. But during the middleware execution, unexpected problems may happen and the sent response may be in error status, which is annoying because the client thinks his request failed while it was stored inside the DB. So closing the session at the end of the middleware would have been a good solution, so this is a shame that this feature doesn't work anymore :/.

The only workaround I found is to not use the `yield` feature, but to create, close and manage the commit/rollback of the session inside the middleware, and to store its instance into `Request.state` object to share it between the middleware and the endpoint method, then a `starlette_context` (https://starlette-context.readthedocs.io/en/latest/fastapi.html) allows to inject the session in any method/function without passing the session as argument. FYI the `starlette_context` is not available from the middleware method, like the `Depends` of FastApi.

Example:
```py
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, FastAPI, Request
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session
from starlette_context import context, request_cycle_context

# The starlette context feature, init a new context for each request/response cycle:
async def my_context_dependency():
    data = {}
    with request_cycle_context(data):
        yield

app = FastAPI(dependencies=[Depends(my_context_dependency)])


@app.middleware("http")
async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):

    session: Final[Session] = db.createSession() # method of your own to create a new Session instance, no yield inside.
    request.state.session = session # store the session inside the request to share it with the endpoint method.

    request.state.any_data = ... # store anything you want, it will be available for the method of the endpoint.

    try:
        response = await call_next(request) # call the endpoint method.

        any_other_data = request.state.any_other_data # Exists because it has been set in the endpoint method.

        session.commit()
    except Exception as e:
        session.rollback()
        raise e
    finally:
        session.close()

    return response

# Optional method, but useful if you want to add the session inside the Fastapi injectable dependencies.
def get_session(request: Request) -> Session:
    return request.state.session # Exists because it has been set by the middleware method.

# Task launched at the end of the endpoint by using the BackgroundTasks feature (see below), after having sent the response to the client (the last response for streaming response)
def end_task(any_data, any_other_data, ...):
    ...

# The endpoint method:
app.post("")
def endpoint(
    request: Request,
    background_task: BackgroundTasks,
    session: Session = Depends(get_session) # Optional. If you don't use this injection, the session is inside request.state.session set by the middleware.
):
    context["session"] = session # Store the session inside the starlette context.

    response = db_service() # Defined in another module (see below), no need to pass the session as argument.
    
    any_data = request.state.any_data # exists because it has been set in the middleware method.

    request.state.any_other_data = ... # store inside `request.state` anything you want, it will be available in the middleware when returning to it on response.

    background_task.add_task(end_task, any_data, any_other_data,...) # you can pass anything you want

    return response


### In another Python module for the db services
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session
from starlette_context import context

def db_service(session: Session = None): # impossible to pass the context as default value, it generates an error during the import of the module, because it exists only during a request/response cycle.
    if session is None: # if no session is given in arg, use the one of the context of the current endpoint
        session = context["session"]

    # Any commands in DB
    ...

    return result

```

I think you can find a similar solution for your problems of stream.

**To summarize:**
- you don't need to use the `yield` feature.
- share data between the middleware and the endpoint by the `request.state`, it works both ways (ie. you can also set a new field in `request.state` from the endpoint method, it will be available in the middleware when returning to it on response).
- use starlette context to retrieve anything you want from anywhere during a request/response cycle.
- use BackgroundTasks to pass and launch anything you want after the sending of the response (the sending of the last response for streaming response) 

For example, I could have managed the commit/rollback/closing of the db session from a background task if I want to continue to use the session's transaction after the sending of the response. Or if only some endpoints needs a db session, you don't have to create all the sessions from the middleware, but from the endpoint method needing it, and send it to the middleware or to a background task as explained to manage its commit/rollback/closing (these operations have to be done only if a session has been sent by the endpoint, so then it works for any endpoint even if it doesn't create and send a session).

Regards.

I got bit by this when using `psycopg.AsyncConnection` with `StreamingResponse`.

I want to use a pattern like described in this [article](https://blog.danielclayton.co.uk/posts/database-connections-with-fastapi/#add-on-using-a-psycopg-connection-pool) where my connections is declared using `Depends`:

```python
import os
from typing import Annotated

import psycopg
import uvicorn
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

app = FastAPI()


async def get_conn():
    async with await psycopg.AsyncConnection.connect(
        "postgresql://user:pass@localhost/db"
    ) as conn:
        yield conn


conn_dep = Annotated[psycopg.AsyncConnection, Depends(get_conn)]


@app.get("/")
async def stream(conn: conn_dep):
    async with conn.cursor() as cursor:

        async def _stream():
            await cursor.execute("SELECT 42")
            row = await cursor.fetchone()
            yield f"data: {row}\n\n"

        return StreamingResponse(_stream(), media_type="text/event-stream")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=int(os.getenv("PORT", "8000")), reload=True)
```

But, I get the following error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "starlette/responses.py", line 264, in wrap
    await func()
  File "starlette/responses.py", line 245, in stream_response
    async for chunk in self.body_iterator:
  File "test.py", line 27, in _stream
    await cursor.execute("SELECT 42")
  File "psycopg/cursor_async.py", line 97, in execute
    raise ex.with_traceback(None)
psycopg.OperationalError: the connection is closed
```

Since I want to continue to use the latest version of FastAPI I restructured my code to manually manage the connection:

```python
import os

import psycopg
import uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/")
async def stream():
    conn = await psycopg.AsyncConnection.connect("postgresql://user:pass@localhost/db")
    cursor = conn.cursor()

    async def _stream():
        try:
            await cursor.execute("SELECT 42")
            row = await cursor.fetchone()
            yield f"data: {row}\n\n"
        finally:
            await cursor.close()
            await conn.close()

    return StreamingResponse(_stream(), media_type="text/event-stream")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=int(os.getenv("PORT", "8000")), reload=True)
```
Is there any update on this, we've been stuck on version 0.105 for 18 months at this point...

@tiangolo  Just trying to understand the thinking on this one. This seems like Fast API is dying in a sense that no progress is being made on a pretty fundamental broken part of the system, should users just take this as this will never work and we should find alternatives to Fast API?

lifetime topic is as hard as in rust.

My two cents here:
- One way to solve the issue is to consume (await) the response object in get_request_handler(), but the background task may occupy the context and resources indefinitely.
- Other way is to inspect the ASGI message, and manage the async_exit_stack manually, so that the stack is closed after `more_body=False`.

Both need big refactor, and probably cause incompatibilities.

Lastly, if the dependency chain is simple, we may inject the database connection factory into the streaming response. and create a new database object there. (of course, this assume the lifetime of the factory (and its dependencies) outlive the request handler, and this is usually true because the factory is usable during process lifetime).

```python
engine = create_engine(DB_URI)
Session = sessionmaker(bind=engine)

def get_db_factory() -> Callable[[], Session]:
   return Session

def iterator(
    db_factory,
) -> Iterator[str]:
   db = db_factory()
   with db:
     yield 'hello'
     db.commit()
     yield 'world'
     db.commit()

@router.get("/stream")
def hello(  
    db_factory = Depends(get_db_factory),
) -> StreamingResponse:
    return StreamingResponse(iterator(db_factory))
```
Support for `StreamingResponse`s using dependencies with `yield` should be fixed by this: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14099 (once it's merged and released). 🚀
Amazing!
FastAPI 0.118.0 was just released with the fix in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14099. 🐛 🚀 🎉

issues.title: Potential footgun when using custom `Response(background=<Task()>)` in conjunction with injected `BackgroundTasks` - the custom response overwrites the tasks
issues.body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/11214

<div type='discussions-op-text'>

<sup>Originally posted by **netanel-haber** February 28, 2024</sup>
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title here.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, BackgroundTasks
from starlette.responses import Response, BackgroundTask
import uvicorn

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
async def endpoint(tasks: BackgroundTasks):
    tasks.add_task(lambda: print("This won't be printed"))
    return Response(content="Custom response", background=BackgroundTask(lambda: print("Only this will be printed")))

uvicorn.run(app)
```


### Description

Hey! Thanks for the best framework in Python and generally ❤️ 

Basically, passing a background task to a custom response overwrites any other tasks added to the injected `BackgroundTasks`.

I'm not sure this is considered a bug, but I think it's confusing and can at least be clarified in the [Return a Response Directly](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/response-directly/) docs, for example. Other solutions can be actually changing this behavior (I guess that's a breaking change), or printing a warning/throwing [throwing is als
issues.author: Kludex

comments.body: I've created the issue because I think we can do better than just ignoring those background tasks. Maybe we can add them to the returned `Response` object?

That's what I alluded to when I mentioned:

> ...actually changing this behavior

Meaning, somehow making it so both the injected tasks, and the passed background are executed as expected. For example, is it possible to append thebackground task to the injected tasks? Does FastAPI/Starlette provide a way to get the injections for a given request? Is that available to the Response class (and inheritors like StreamingResponse)?

This is a breaking change because existing apps might find that behavior suddenly changes (=tasks that weren't running before due to this overwrite suddenly run) - like I mentioned, this "breaking change" is really fixing a behavior that doesn't really make sense. But it kind of depends on the repo's policy, I think.

I'd be happy to contribute a pr for this, either way.

Adding this because it seems related. It is certainly a source of an incredibly obscure bug that had me scratching my head for days. There's nothing in FastAPI that stops you caching a frequently used `Response` in a variable:
```python
RESPONSE = HTMLResponse('ok')
```
If you return such a response from an endpoint that adds a background task you get a very obscure bug. This particular task takes a string argument. The first time you call the endpoint everything seems ok. The second time you call it, the background task is invoked with the same argument value as first time. I presume this is because of the same smoke and mirrors noted by @netanel-haber, whereby the `Response` is actually used to stash the background task context.

Here's a fully worked example:

```python
# wtest.py
import uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI, BackgroundTasks
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse
import random
import string

RESPONSE = HTMLResponse('ok')

app = FastAPI()

def random_string():
    letters = string.ascii_lowercase
    return ''.join(random.choice(letters) for i in range(20))

async def background_task(arg: str) -> None:
    print(f"{arg=}")

@app.get("/api", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def api(bk: BackgroundTasks) -> str:
    param = random_string()
    print(f"{param=}")
    bk.add_task(background_task, param)
    return RESPONSE

if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(
        "wtest:app",
        host="0.0.0.0",
        port=80,
        log_level="info",
        reload=True,
    )
```

Here's the output when you invoke `/api` twice. `param` is the parameter passed to the background task and `arg` is the argument as seen by the task. They should match up. Second time round, they don't:
```
INFO:     Application startup complete.
param='cmvlzpyrzyzddhmwikri'
INFO:     127.0.0.1:53216 - "GET /api HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
arg='cmvlzpyrzyzddhmwikri'
param='quomuzuwsbjvfqsjamrx'
INFO:     127.0.0.1:53216 - "GET /api HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
arg='cmvlzpyrzyzddhmwikri'```
```

> There's nothing in FastAPI that stops you caching a frequently used Response in a variable:
> 
> ```py
> RESPONSE = HTMLResponse('ok')
> ...
> 
> @app.get("/api", response_class=HTMLResponse)
> async def api(bk: BackgroundTasks) -> str:
>     param = random_string()
>     print(f"{param=}")
>     bk.add_task(background_task, param)
>     return RESPONSE
> ```

I think we should add a warning in docs that Response-class instances should be one-time objects and they shouldn't be re-used.

As for initial issue, I can't say for sure what the correct approach is.

Combining background tasks of temporary response with background task of actual response looks intuitive for me, but it might be a breaking change.

The solution might be to add a warning [here](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/d5d302c3da11262f4e320a9af5dbb9d13386e1b5/fastapi/routing.py#L307-L310) in such cases:

```
                    if isinstance(raw_response, Response):
                        if raw_response.background is None:
                            raw_response.background = solved_result.background_tasks
                        elif solved_result.background_tasks is not None:
                            warnings.warn(
                                "background parameter of actual response overrides the "
                                "background tasks of temporary response object",
                                UserWarning,
                            )
                        response = raw_response
```

This way it will not happen silently and user can easily fix this by adding task to temporary response object instead of passing it as a `background` parameter.

The correct solution to this issue is clear to me: FastAPI should _never_ mutate the response objects we return (or assume any of their fields are initialized). That is a clear overstepping of its boundaries.

> we should add a warning in docs that Response-class instances should be one-time objects

That's not a Starlette requirement, so FastAPI should not impose this artificial constraint upon the developer either. Static, reusable responses (e.g. 404s or other static content) are quite handy and pre-creating them decreases overhead.

Instead, there is a very simple solution FastAPI could implement to fix this issue:

```python
class ResponseWithExtraBackground(Response):
    def __init__(self, raw_response, background):
        self.raw_response = raw_response
        self.background = background

    async def __call__(scope, receive, send):
        await self.raw_response.__call__(scope, receive, send)

        # Same implementation as in starlette's Response:
        await self.background()

# later...

if isinstance(raw_response, Response):
    if solved_result.background_tasks is None:
        response = raw_response
    else:
        response = ResponseWithExtraBackground(raw_response, solved_result.background_tasks)
```

This is a better implementation for a variety of reasons, not the least of which is now FastAPI won't be making the assumption that `background` is even initialized on the Response object in the first place (which it might not be for custom Response subclasses), or that a custom Response subclass will even call `await self.background()` at all (which it might not because the developer doesn't realize FastAPI is hijacking one of its fields).

issues.title: Breaking change with path parameters when updating to pydantic>=2 from pydantic<2
issues.body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/11250
Originally posted by **ThirVondukr** March 5, 2024 ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title here. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python from uuid import UUID from fastapi import FastAPI app = FastAPI() @app.get("/int/{path}") async def int_(path: int | str): return str(type(path)) @app.get("/uuid/{path}") async def uuid_(path: UUID | str): return str(type(path)) ``` ### Description With pydantic<2 parameters are correctly parsed as int and UUID if an appropriate string is passed, with pydantic 2 they're always interpreted as str, I presume this will work the same way with all other types that you can pass into path parameters. I also observed the same behavior with query, so I assume it behaves the same way with all FastAPI parameters (headers, body, etc). ### Operating System Windows ### Operating System Details _No response_ ### FastAPI Version 0.110.0 ### Pydantic Version 1.10.14 / 2.6.3 ### Python Version Python 3.11.5 ### Additional Context _No response_
issues.author: Kludex

comments.body:
A workaround for anyone facing this (NB: this assumes availability of typing.Annotated and pydantic.Field):
```diff
@app.get("/uuid/{path}")
-async def uuid_(path: UUID | str):
+async def uuid_(path: Annotated[UUID | str, Field(union_mode='left_to_right')]):
    return str(type(path))
```

Pydantic v2 switched to union_mode='smart' as the default, which in cases like this will always prefer str (since it's an exact type match).

It's also necessary to do this in any dependencies (as far as I can tell, type annotations set on direct path parameters and function parameters of dependencies aren't shared).

Based on the GitHub issue, the problem is:

In Pydantic v1 (version 1.10.14 mentioned in the details), path parameters with type annotations like int and UUID would correctly parse string inputs into those types when passed through URL paths.
After upgrading to Pydantic v2 (version 2.6.3), all path parameters are always interpreted as strings, regardless of the type annotation.

Looking at the example code:

`@app.get("/int/{path}")
async def int_(path: int | str):
    return str(type(path))

@app.get("/uuid/{path}")
async def uuid_(path: UUID | str):
    return str(type(path))`

With Pydantic v1, if you called /int/123, the path parameter would be parsed as an integer. With Pydantic v2, it's always a string.
This is indeed a breaking change that affects type validation and conversion in FastAPI applications. The issue reporter suspects this behavior applies to all FastAPI parameters (path, query, headers, body, etc.).
The most direct solution would be to manually convert the types in your route handlers:

`@app.get("/int/{path}")
async def int_(path: str):
    try:
        path_as_int = int(path)
        # Use path_as_int
    except ValueError:
        # Handle invalid format
    return str(type(path))`

Alternatively, you might want to check if there are configuration options in Pydantic v2 or FastAPI to maintain backward compatibility with type parsing behavior.
If you're maintaining a large codebase with many endpoints, you might consider using FastAPI dependency injection to create reusable converters that can be applied across multiple endpoints.

As I see it, we don't need to fix this (that would cause even more confusion, and this will be breaking change), but we should add a note about this with a [solution suggested by **H-Plus-Time**](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/11251#issuecomment-2024267760) to docs.
Probably [here](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/python-types/#union), but maybe better add a dedicated _"Input parameters type validation"_ section in Tutorial (I guess there are other specific cases we can describe there as well)

I think @H-Plus-Time answer solves this. This is a change in how Pydantic works, I think it makes sense to "respect" Pydantic's behavior, given they provide a way to customize it if needed, instead of doing workarounds here.

So, I'll close this one one, thanks all! ☕

comments.body:
I made a PR for that at #11867
There was a little typo I suppose when passing the parameters to the pydantic object
This should be solved by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/11867, the fix will be available in FastAPI `0.112.2` released in the next few hours. :tada:

### Commit to Help

### Example Code

```python
# This is just the code from https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt/#update-the-dependencies

from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from typing import Annotated

import jwt
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, status

# to get a string like this run:

issues.author: Kludex

comments.body:
@Kludex I don't know if this is the correct way, but we can change [this code](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/security/oauth2.py#L64-L75) by replacing the form pattern with the default value. Something like this

```python
grant_type: Annotated[
    Union[str, None],
    Form(),
    Doc(
        """
        The OAuth2 spec says it is required and MUST be the fixed string
        "password". Nevertheless, this dependency class is permissive and
        allows not passing it. If you want to enforce it, use instead the
        `OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict` dependency.
        """
    ),
] = "password",
```

After that change, everything works with the default or empty value.
Is there any objection to this proposed fix?

Investigating this report, I was able to trace the origin of the problem and opened a new discussion here https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12536 as it broadly concerns Forms.

I can confirm and have verified that the issue exists and that the proposed solution could be applied to mitigate the problem. However, I do not agree with accepting this solution as a fix for two reasons:

1. The cause of the issue is not within the OAuth2PasswordRequestForm class.
2. The relaxed value of that field is fixed to two possible values: password, which is specific to OAuth, and None, which constitutes the relaxed part but is easily associated with the name in the class. 
In the proposed solution, however, the grant_type field could be associated with any value.
This was fixed by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/13537 and available in FastAPI 0.123.2 🎉

issues.title: Optional form field not working with test client
issues.body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12227

<div type='discussions-op-text'>

<sup>Originally posted by **MartinAchtnerAA** September 19, 2024</sup>
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title here.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from typing import Annotated, Literal, Optional

from fastapi import FastAPI, Form
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/")
async def read_main(
    attribute : Annotated[Optional[Literal["abc", "def"]], Form()]
):
    print(attribute)


client = TestClient(app)


data = {}
data["attribute"] = None

response = client.post("/", data=data)
print(response.content)
assert response.status_code == 200
```


### Description

In FastAPI 0.113 .0 the above code passes.
Since version 0.114.0 the response is a 422 status code with error message '{"detail":[{"type":"literal_error","loc":["body","attribute"],"msg":"Input should be \'abc\' or \'def\'","input":"","ctx":{"expected":"\'abc\' or \'def\'"}}]}'

### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.114.0

### Pydantic Version

2.9.1

### Python Version

3.12.3

### Additional Conte
issues.author: Kludex

comments.body: My notes about this issue are written here: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12227#discussioncomment-10716549
@Kludex I extended your example to add some more context to the issue in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12227#discussioncomment-10749794

Hi everyone,

we ran into a very similar issue and were able narrow the issue down to the behaviour of the `TestClient`.

The issue occured when we were updating to FastApi 0.114.1. Previously, we were on the rather old version 0.78, therefore I cannot say for certain with which update the problem occurred, based on the post above probably on 0.113 -> 0.114.1

Here are the details we identified as the cause for the issue:

The behaviour of the `TestClient` changed regarding header and body with None-values. The expected behaviour, in my opinion, is that the `TestClient` behaves like the `requests` module:

```
import requests

url = "http://your-api/endpoint/"
params = {"x1": "foo", "x2": None}´
r = requests.get(url, params=params)

print(r.request.url)
>>> 'http://your-api/endpoint?x1=foo'
```

When using the `TestClient` from the older release, the behaviour was as expected:
```
from fastapi.test_client import TestClient

url = "/endpoint/"
params = {"x1": "foo", "x2": None}´
r = test_client.get(url, params=params)

print(r.request.url)
>>> 'http://testserver/endpoint?x1=foo'
```

However, when using the fastapi 0.114.1 the behaviour changes to:
```
from fastapi.test_client import TestClient

url = "/endpoint/"
params = {"x1": "foo", "x2": None}´
r = test_client.get(url, params=params)

print(r.request.url)
>>> 'http://testserver/endpoint?x1=foo&x2='
```

Due to that, the endpoint receives `""` as value for `x2` and possibly raises a Pydantic error, or worse uses the empty string instead of default value.

This is a change in behaviour, I did not find anything in the release notes about. I doubt that the behaviour is intended this way.


Best regards
Christian

Hi everyone,
I also encountered this issue. However, for me the example is failing also with FastAPI 0.113.0 (starlette 0.38.6, pydantic 2.9.2). The error is slightly different than the one reported:

```
{"detail":[{"type":"missing","loc":["body","attribute"],"msg":"Field required","input":null}]}
```

Thanks for your efforts 🙏

Hi all! I'm facing the same issue as well using the httpx AsyncClient for testing:

```python
import asyncio
from typing import Optional
from uuid import UUID

from fastapi import FastAPI, Form
from httpx import AsyncClient

app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/")
async def test_optional(
    test_id: Optional[UUID] = Form(None, alias="testId"),
) -> None:
    print(f"Test ID is {test_id}")


async def main() -> None:
    async with AsyncClient(app=app, base_url="http://dummy") as client:
        data = {"testId": None}
        response = await client.post("/", data=data)
        print(response.content)
        assert response.status_code == 200


if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
```

Up until `fastapi-slim==0.113.0` this works, starting from `fastapi-slim==0.114.0` this fails with a 400 response `{"detail":[{"loc":["body","testId"],"msg":"value is not a valid uuid","type":"type_error.uuid"}]}`

After a first investigation it looks like the issue can be traced back to [this MR](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12134/files#). The add [assignment loop](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/febf6b6a52e36e0d64920fb54670ad7fc29b2eaa/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L862) seems to be breaking in this case:
- First, the value for `testId` gets fetched, detected as an empty string and treated in the same way as a None value [here](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/febf6b6a52e36e0d64920fb54670ad7fc29b2eaa/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L718) which makes it provide the default value [here](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/febf6b6a52e36e0d64920fb54670ad7fc29b2eaa/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L728) (None, in this case)
- However, None values are skipped [here](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/febf6b6a52e36e0d64920fb54670ad7fc29b2eaa/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L860)
- But then it get's added anyway as the raw value (the empty string) [here]( https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/febf6b6a52e36e0d64920fb54670ad7fc29b2eaa/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L864)

Thanks in advance for the efforts on this 🙏
I proposed a pull request here trying to differentiate between None and not specified: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12502
Nice work! Thank you
I see there is a PR fixing the issue since 2 months. Is there a reason to keep this hanging? :astonished:
I am also waiting for https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12502

It seems like this structure works as workaround:

```
OptionalUUID = Annotated[Optional[UUID], BeforeValidator(lambda v: UUID(v) if v else None)]

@app.post("/")
async def read_main(
    attribute : Annotated[Optional[OptionalUUID], Form()] = None
):
    print(attribute)

```

I think @Kludex's answer in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12227#discussioncomment-10716549 answers this.

`Optional` doesn't really mean "optional": https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/python-types/#using-union-or-optional

So, what makes something actually optional is the default value, which is Marcelo's answer there.

So, I'll now close this one. ☕

issues.title: OpenAPI servers not being returned according how the docs say they should be
issues.body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12226
Originally posted by **mzealey** September 19, 2024 ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title here. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python from fastapi import FastAPI app = FastAPI() # you can add a test endpoint here or not - same bug either way ``` ### Description ``` $ curl localhost:8$ curl localhost:8000/openapi.json {"openapi":"3.1.0","info":{"title":"FastAPI","version":"0.1.0"},"paths":{}} ``` According to the documentation of the `servers` parameter in FastAPI: > If the servers list is not provided, or is an empty list, the default value would be a dict with a url value of /. (assuming that `root_path_in_servers = True` (the default)) Clearly this is not happening. ### Operating System Linux ### Operating System Details _No response_ ### FastAPI Version 0.110.3 (but according to github code seems to be in latest also) ### Pydantic Version 2.5.3 ### Python Version Python 3.10.12 ### Additional Context _No response_
issues.author: Kludex

comments.body: Relevant part of the docstrings: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/9606b916ef7c883f5ebeac1bf3db9adf5ae646a9/fastapi/applications.py#L299-L300

@Kludex I don't know if this is the correct way, but we can add default value of `[{"url": "/"}]` instead of `None`. Something like this
```python
servers: Annotated[
    Optional[List[Dict[str, Union[str, Any]]]],
    Doc(
        """
        # unchanged docstring
        """
    ),
] = [{"url": "/"}],
```
Result for default:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()
```
is 
```bash
> curl localhost:8000/openapi.json
{"openapi":"3.1.0","info":{"title":"FastAPI","version":"0.1.0"},"servers":[{"url":"/"}],"paths":{}}
```
Result for provided urls:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI(
    servers=[
        {"url": "https://stag.example.com", "description": "Staging environment"},
        {"url": "https://prod.example.com", "description": "Production environment"},
    ]
)
```
is

```bash
> curl localhost:8000/openapi.json
{"openapi":"3.1.0","info":{"title":"FastAPI","version":"0.1.0"},"servers":[{"url":"https://stag.example.com","description":"Staging environment"},{"url":"https://prod.example.com","description":"Production environment"}],"paths":{}}
```

Interestingly I see at https://swagger.io/docs/specification/api-host-and-base-path/:

> If the servers array is not provided or is empty, the server URL defaults to /:

But a lot of readers don't seem to do this so probably worth fully specifying it in FastAPI.

> Relevant part of the docstrings:
> 
> [fastapi/fastapi/applications.py](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/9606b916ef7c883f5ebeac1bf3db9adf5ae646a9/fastapi/applications.py#L299-L300)
> 
> Lines 299 to 300 in [9606b91](/fastapi/fastapi/commit/9606b916ef7c883f5ebeac1bf3db9adf5ae646a9)
> 
>                  If the servers `list` is not provided, or is an empty `list`, the 
>                  default value would be a `dict` with a `url` value of `/`.

I think that current docstring is not entirely correct. It will default to mounting point of the app, not just `'/'`.

And, it does work correctly for sub-applications:

```py
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()
sub = FastAPI()

app.mount("/sub", sub)
```

**http://localhost:8000/sub/openapi.json**

```json
{
  "openapi": "3.1.0",
  "info": {
    "title": "FastAPI",
    "version": "0.1.0"
  },
  "servers": [
    {
      "url": "/sub"  // Not just `/`, but mounting point of sub-app
    }
  ],
  "paths": {
  }
}
```

And with `root_path` as well:

```py
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI(root_path="/api")
```

**http://localhost:8000/api/openapi.json**

```json
{
  "openapi": "3.1.0",
  "info": {
    "title": "FastAPI",
    "version": "0.1.0"
  },
  "servers": [
    {
      "url": "/api"  // Not just `/`, but mounting point of app (root_path)
    }
  ],
  "paths": {

  }
}
```

At the same time, `servers` is [optional according to openapi spec](url). So, it's ok to omit it if the mounting point is `'/'`.

So, I think everything works correctly and we just need to fix docstring to something like:
```
                If the servers `list` is not provided, or is an empty `list`, the
                default value would be a `dict` with a `url` value of mounting point of this application.
```

We can probably explain this in more details in docs and add a link to this docstring
This should be solved by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14405, thanks all! 🍰

comments.body:
The documentation is wrong.

This is the file that is testing that part of the documentation: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/529155e72e196f82e0289f173a185e8ddb2888ce/tests/test_tutorial/test_query_params/test_tutorial006_py310.py

As we can see, it doesn't pass the `None`...
This may be the same as https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/12245

> Hi [@Kludex](https://github.com/Kludex), should i open a PR to `improve documentation` like below one, this will be my first open source contribution?
> 
> ```
> @app.get("/items/")
> async def read_items(q: Annotated[str | None, Query()]):  # required
>     if q == "null":
>         q = None
> 
>     results = {"items": [{"item_id": "Foo"}, {"item_id": "Bar"}]}
>     if q is not None:
>         results.update({"q": q})
>     return results
> ```

"Don't ask for permission, ask for forgiveness"

Hi everyone, I've reproduced this on my local machine. When using Annotated[Union[str, None], Query(min_length=3)] = ..., the Swagger UI marks the field as Required and won't allow execution if it's empty. This confirms the confusion mentioned in the 'Required, can be None' section of the docs. I'm interested in helping fix this in the documentation. Should we update the table to clarify that ... always renders as required in the UI regardless of the type?
@khuloud0, see https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14322

Hey everyone! 👋 

I was looking into this same confusion today and found this issue. After digging around, I discovered that there's already an open PR #14322 by @ChaitanyaSai-Meka that addresses exactly this documentation problem.

The PR clarifies that HTTP query parameters are transmitted as strings, so clients can't actually send Python's  or JSON  directly. It adds helpful examples showing how to handle cases where you want to accept a "None" string or empty string as  in your route.

**Current status of the fix:**
- ✅ Reviewed by @YuriiMotov (maintainer)
- ✅ All feedback has been addressed
- ⏳ Waiting for final review by Sebastian
- ⚠️ Has merge conflicts that need resolving

For anyone blocked by this in the meantime, the workaround is:
1. Use  without  if the parameter is optional
2. If you need to accept literal "None" or empty strings as , handle that conversion inside your route or use Pydantic's  (v2 only)

@ChaitanyaSai-Meka - thanks for putting together this PR! The documentation clarification will definitely help a lot of people. Would you like help resolving the merge conflicts, or are you all set there? 😊

Hope this helps anyone else who stumbles upon this issue!

@YuriiMotov @vincent067 Thanks for the update @vincent067! It’s great to know that a PR (#14322) is already addressing this. I’ll follow the progress there to learn how the documentation will be clarified. Appreciate the workarounds you mentioned!

Hey @vincent067 , thnks a lot for th kind words and for pointing ths out! 
I just checked again,  it looks like there are no merge conflicts on the branch anymore (GitHub shows it can be cleanly merged).
Pls let me know if you’re seeing anything different on your end

issues.title: Will FastAPI support QUERY http method? "app.query"
issues.body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/6049

<div type='discussions-op-text'>

<sup>Originally posted by **FilipeMarch** December 16, 2022</sup>
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()


@app.query('/query/subjects')
def query_subjects(schema: ArbitrarySchema):
    with Session(engine) as db:
        subjects = db.query(Subject).all()
        return schema(**subjects)

# something like this
```


### Description

There is a new HTTP method called QUERY, I discovered it this week and it is super interesting!

https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-httpbis-safe-method-w-body-02.html

I was trying to make a GET route that would accept a request body, but started receiving an error on Swagger UI:

[TypeError: Request has method 'GET' and cannot have a body](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui/issues/5891#issuecomment-1340591857)

The idea is that sometimes we need to make a big or complex query, and this is the scenario in which we can see the advantages of GraphQL, and although I have seen that [we can integrate FastAPI with GraphQL](https://fastapi.tiango
issues.author: Kludex

Has this been claimed? I see a PR out but not reviewed. If it's not been actively worked on, I'd love to claim it and take a stab at it :)

OpenAPI 3.2 [was just released](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/releases/tag/3.2.0) with official support for the QUERY method AND a generic support for additional http methods.

Not only will we be able to support QUERY, we should be able to support any HTTP method we might want.

### Discussed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/8428

<div type='discussions-op-text'>

<sup>Originally posted by **NewSouthMjos** December  5, 2022</sup>
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [x] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
import uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends


app = FastAPI()


def get_something_sync():
    yield True


async def get_something_async():
    yield True


@app.get('/1')
def router_func(dependency=Depends(get_something_sync)):
    raise ValueError
    return


@app.get('/2')
def router_func(dependency=Depends(get_something_async)):
    raise ValueError
    return


if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run("main:app", host="0.0.0.0", port=5600, workers=1)
```


### Description

If dependency function uses yield, injecting dependency with sync def function (get_something_sync), the error traceback will broke - it doesn't locate the place where it was rise (should be "/app/main.py", line 24)
calling endpoint /1:
```
fastapi_dependency  | INFO:     10.77.78.83:60070 - "GET /1 HTTP/1.1" 500 Internal Server Error
fastapi_dependency  | ERROR:    Exception in ASGI application
fastapi_dependency  | Traceback

issues.title: Migrate questions in GitHub Issues to GitHub Discussions
issues.body:
### Privileged issue

- [X] I'm @tiangolo or he asked me directly to create an issue here.

### Issue Content

This is just to describe the process of migrating the GitHub issues with questions (with the label `question`) to GitHub Discussions.

Not sure if this would be useful to anyone else, maybe just for me to come back to this if I ever need it. :shrug: 

## Current GitHub Support

GitHub currently has a feature to migrate issues with a label to GitHub Discussions.

What it does is:

* For each open issue with that label, create the equivalent discussion, with all the comments, by the same users, etc.
* The issue still exists, apart from the discussion, but is then closed and locked. And the UI shows a link to the one in GitHub Discussions.
    * This information, the fact that it was migrated and that there's now a corresponding discussion, is not available in the APIs (not in REST, not in GraphQL).
    * An alternative that worked in my case, but not necessarily in others, is to check for locked issues. This worked for me only because I had never locked an issue, and when GitHub migrates them, it locks them.

But it has several catches:

* It only migrates open issues. So, questions that were answered and closed are not migrated.
* There's no API, no REST, no GraphQL. And for each individual issue, there's a link to migrate to Discussions, but still, it doesn't seem to use the API, seems to be a hardcoded form with a backend-rendered token (probably?). So, not easy to automate that way.
* When trying to migrate all the issues with the label `question`, it seems that was crashing GitHub servers, I was getting a 502 error. :shrug: So, I can't migrate everything in a single pass.

More details here: https://github.com/community/community/discussions/48503

## Filtering Issues

### Context About Issue Types

When I had enabled issue reports for "bugs" and "feature requests", it was common for people to open "bugs" that were actually err

issues.title: 🐛 Type annotations for headers in `HTTPException` declare `Any` as values but only accept `str`
issues.body:
🐛 Type annotations for headers in `HTTPException` declare `Any` as values but only accept `str`.

Fixed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/9648

### Discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/8562

<div type='discussions-op-text'>

<sup>Originally posted by **DorAbu** January 20, 2022</sup>
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/")
async def root():
    raise HTTPException(
        status_code=503,
        detail=f"The server is not ready to handle the request, due to ...",
        headers={"Retry-After": 20}
    )
```


### Description

In HTTPException object signature (attached below 1.),  headers type hint is Optional[Dict[str, Any]].
But for example, when Dict[str, int] is given as in the "example code" section, the implementation in Starlette will raise an exception - AttributeError: 'int' object has no attribute 'encode' (attached below 2.).

Seems that Starlette implementation expects Optional[Dict[str, str]] type hint, FastAPI may resolve it by converting the values of the headers to strings.

1. ![image](https://user-ima
issues.author: tiangolo

I think it depends on starlette. Thats core dependency for FastAPI, and still doesn't provide major release. While integrating MongoDB, which uses BSON instead of the JSON type of data makes the retrieved data not possible to iterate over, forcing the BSON serialization to String (Only the _id of the inserted items are BSON).

FastAPI `0.99.1`
Pydantic `1.10.11` 

With these versions of libraries, it was possible to make such a serialization with the following line of code:

```python
pydantic.json.ENCODERS_BY_TYPE[ObjectId] = str
```

while importing the bson object, which is not separately installed but came with the pymongo[srv] installation alongside.

```python
from bson import ObjectId
```

Sadly, this conversion is completely broken by both @pydantic and @fastapi with their latest releases.

@tiangolo , after upgrade to `fastapi==0.100.0` and `pydantic==2.0.2` I got following issues below. Both worked fine with `fastapi<0.100.0` and `pydantic==1.10.11`.
See MRE:
```python
from collections.abc import Sequence
from typing import Annotated, Any, Generic, TypeVar

from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel, TypeAdapter
from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass as pydantic_dc

api = FastAPI()


fake_items_db = [{"item_name": "Foo"}, {"item_name": "Bar"}, {"item_name": "Baz"}]


T = TypeVar('T')


class ItemOut(BaseModel):
    item_name: str


class PaginationOut(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    page: int
    per_page: int
    pages: int
    total: int
    items: Sequence[T]

    @classmethod
    def model_parametrized_name(cls, params: tuple[type[Any], ...]) -> str:
        return f'{params[0].__name__.removesuffix("Out")}{cls.__name__}'


@pydantic_dc()
class CommonQueryParams:
    q: str | None = None
    skip: int = 0
    limit: int = 100


@api.get("/items/")
async def read_items(
    commons: Annotated[CommonQueryParams, Depends(CommonQueryParams)]
) -> PaginationOut[ItemOut]:
    pagination = PaginationOut[ItemOut](
        page=1,
        per_page=1,
        pages=1,
        total=1,
        items=TypeAdapter(list[ItemOut]).validate_python(fake_items_db)
    )
    return PaginationOut[ItemOut].model_validate(pagination)
```
1. Query parameters turned into `args` and `kwargs`:
![image](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/assets/54511144/b78cd172-ced1-4fea-af5a-389738858d90)
2. Incorrect definition for `PaginationOut[ItemOut]` model. The name should be `ItemPaginationOut` (the class defined `model_parametrized_name`) and pagination attributes should be of proper type instead of `any`:
![image](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/assets/54511144/6670bbf6-eeb2-433d-ab7d-8618c2a4b3f8)
Thanks!

Issue 1. Seems like the `pydantic` issue, since the it is generating the following signature:
`<Signature (*args: 'Any', **kwargs: 'Any') -> 'None'>`. Whereas the stdlib dataclasses generate the signature as expected.

We're seeing an issue with pydantic version `2.0.2` as well. When looking at the dependency list for `0.100.0` it's pretty apparent why pip installs this version though:

```
dependencies = [
    "starlette>=0.27.0,<0.28.0",
    "pydantic>=1.7.4,!=1.8,!=1.8.1,!=2.0.0,!=2.0.1,<3.0.0",
    "typing-extensions>=4.5.0",
]
```

Maybe `2.0.2` should be marked as incompatible together with the rest of the versions. Or maybe use `pydantic~=1.10.0` instead? I know that the `0.100.0` release is explicitly about supporting pydantic v2, but it really seems like it doesn't work / introduces breaking changes.

> 2. Incorrect definition for `PaginationOut[ItemOut]` model. The name should be `ItemPaginationOut` (the class defined `model_parametrized_name`) and pagination attributes should be of proper type instead of `any`:
>    ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/54511144/252432757-6670bbf6-eeb2-433d-ab7d-8618c2a4b3f8.png)
>    Thanks!

What's the compatible code for V1?

EDIT: NVM, the issue is the signature, mentioned on the comment that comes after.

> Issue 1. Seems like the `pydantic` issue, since the it is generating the following signature: `<Signature (*args: 'Any', **kwargs: 'Any') -> 'None'>`. Whereas the stdlib dataclasses generate the signature as expected.

Right. I'm going to take a look.

EDIT: This is solved by https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/6633.

I'll lock this issue. As @tiangolo said, the idea of this issue was to redirect people to the discussion on #9709. 👀 

It's easier to organize the issues if everything is over there. 😅 

If you have an issue with FastAPI + Pydantic V2, please post it on #9709, or create a new discussion. 🙏

issues.title: 🐛 OpenAPI validation errors after upgrading from FastAPI 0.98.0 to 0.99.0, valid JSON Schema includes `bool`
issues.body:
## Description

(by @tiangolo)

The problem is that a "valid JSON Schema" includes a `bool` (i.e. `true` and `false`).

So, `additionalProperties` doesn't have to be a JSON object, it can be `false`, to mean no additional properties are allowed.

When I upgraded the JSON Schema models to include the new types and fields for the new JSON Schema 2020-12 I removed `bool` as a valid JSON Schema.

This is solved in: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/9781

### Discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/9779

<div type='discussions-op-text'>

<sup>Originally posted by **jawnsy** July  1, 2023</sup>
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title here.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
import json
import sys

class FooBaseModel(BaseModel):
    class Config:
        extra = "forbid"

class Foo(FooBaseModel):
    pass

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/")
async def post(
    foo: Foo = None,
):
    pass

if __name__ == "__main__":
    oapi = app.openapi()
    json.dump(oapi, sys.stdout, indent=2)
```


### Description

Upgrading from FastAPI 0.98.0 to 0.99.0 causes OpenAPI failures. The error message looks
issues.author: tiangolo

comments.body: This is solved in FastAPI 0.99.1 🚀

issues.title: Pydantic v2, dataclasses, UUID, and `__annotations__`
issues.body:
### Privileged issue

- [X] I'm @tiangolo or he asked me directly to create an issue here.

### Issue Content

The combination of using:

* Pydantic v2
* dataclasses (instead of Pydantic models)
* UUIDs
* `from future import __annotations__`

seems to break in a strange way.

This was original reported in this discussion by @sanzoghenzo: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/9709#discussioncomment-6449458

In particular the comment by @raddevon with the minimal example by @fantix:

```Python
from __future__ import annotations

import uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Union

from fastapi import FastAPI


@dataclass
class Item:
    id: uuid.UUID
    name: str
    price: float
    tags: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    description: Union[str, None] = None
    tax: Union[float, None] = None


app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/items/next", response_model=Item)
async def read_next_item():
    return {
        "name": "Island In The Moon",
        "price": 12.99,
        "description": "A place to be be playin' and havin' fun",
        "tags": ["breater"],
    }
```

Starting FastAPI with that breaks with an error of:

<details>
<summary>log</summary>

```
uvicorn main:app
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/user/code/fastapi/env3.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic/type_adapter.py", line 165, in __init__
    core_schema = _getattr_no_parents(type, '__pydantic_core_schema__')
  File "/Users/user/code/fastapi/env3.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic/type_adapter.py", line 97, in _getattr_no_parents
    raise AttributeError(attribute)
AttributeError: __pydantic_core_schema__

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/user/code/fastapi/env3.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py", line 625, in _resolve_forward_ref
    obj = _typin
issues.author: tiangolo

Maybe using an id of type str can solve the problem...

class Item:
    id: str  # Using string instead of uuid.UUID
    name: str
    price: float
    tags: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    description: Union[str, None] = None
    tax: Union[float, None] = None

It looks like forward references are resolved from namespaces built from the previous 2 frames
https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/adc657a8b9f0b5191c180fc51005c0bf1fe529db/pydantic/type_adapter.py#L76

You could use Pydantic's dataclass extension. It doesn't just give you the same data validation features but also fix your issue.

```python
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import List, Union
import uuid

from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass, Field

@dataclass
class Item:
    id: uuid.UUID
    name: str
    price: float
    tags: List[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    description: Union[str, None] = None
    tax: Union[float, None] = None

item = Item(
    id=uuid.uuid4(),
    name="John",
    price=666.66,
)

print(item)

# Item(id=UUID('c399d5c9-c2bf-4e5f-94f9-21a7a5f78610'), name='John', price=666.66, tags=[], description=None, tax=None)

This would need to be handled on the Pydantic side (or at least require some input/changes from them). https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/7111

```Python

When I tried to update from:

- `fastapi (0.101.1 -> 0.103.0)`
- `pydantic (1.10.12 -> 2.3.0)`

A route that uses a stdlib `@dataclass` as the return type annotation, started throwing an exception.

```
pydantic.errors.PydanticUndefinedAnnotation: name 'MyDataclass' is not defined
```

`MyDataclass` is defined in another module.

```py
from other.path import MyDataclass

@router.post("/route/")
def route() -> MyDataclass:
    ...
```

In my case, it appears that if any of the nested `@dataclass`es referenced by the top level `MyDataclass` are defined in a module with `from __future__ import annotations`, then this error will be thrown by one of them.

The reason we are using `from __future__ import annotations` is that many of these models use forward references themselves, so this avoids the (IMO ugiler) manually stringified syntax. We try to avoid forward references as much as possible (using `Self` on `@classmethod`s whenever possible, for example) but there are some instances where it can't be avoided (e.g., a normal method that returns a new instance of itself).

There are also weird things that can happen with manually stringified type annotations:

- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72644693

So I figured there was an issue with how the type forward refs were being resolved and I quoted the User references. This led to a very similar error but with `pydantic.errors.PydanticUndefinedAnnotation: name 'Depends' is not defined`.

So, lastly I tried removing the `Annotated` type and set Depends directly as the default value:
```
        @api_router.get("/my-details")
        async def get_my_details(
            requesting_user: User = Depends(self.__controller.get_requesting_user),
        ) -> User:
            # requesting_user = self.__controller.get_item({"id": requesting_user_id})
            logger.info(f"Retrieving user details for user: {requesting_user}")
            return requesting_user
```
This now seems to work fine.

I presume the issue is something to do with when Annotated gets resolved as opposed to defining it as a standard default value. Certainly did have me floundering for a while, I would loved to have seen this mentioned somewhere in the otherwise flawless FastAPI docs (please point me to it if it's already there).

I think my problem is related to this issue, all code comes from document but I can not resolve it.

https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/11423

Hello 👋🏻 

Had a similar issue which was caused by having

```python
from future import __annotations__
```

together with FastAPI dependency injection.

It would result in a weird error

```
PydanticUndefinedAnnotation: name 'Request' is not defined
```

where FastAPI would error on all injected dependencies. Removing the import from `future` fixed the issue.

Thanks.
@andreipetre If the import for `'Request'` is in a `if typing.TYPE_CHECKING:` block it might help moving the import out of that block.
Thanks @Cielquan. That's the weird part, there was no import for `Request` in that file.
This now works with the latest Pydantic versions (probably starting with 2.10), I believe since we refactored forward annotations evaluation.

I can confirm that at least as of

- pydantic v2.11.3
- fastapi v0.115.12
- Python 3.12

We are able to use `from __future__ import annotations` again and avoid manually stringifying type annotations on standard library `@dataclass`es.

The code example from [initial comment](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/10007#issue-1837139184) works well with both, Pydantic V1 and Pydantic V2.
So, the issue has been resolved and can be closed

I added `"id": uuid.uuid4()` to the returning dict to make it pass response validation:

<details>

```py
from __future__ import annotations

import uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Union

from fastapi import FastAPI

@dataclass
class Item:
    id: uuid.UUID
    name: str
    price: float
    tags: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    description: Union[str, None] = None
    tax: Union[float, None] = None

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/items/next", response_model=Item)
async def read_next_item():
    return {
        "id": uuid.uuid4(),
        "name": "Island In The Moon",
        "price": 12.99,
        "description": "A place to be be playin' and havin' fun",
        "tags": ["breater"],
    }
```

</details>

Thanks @YuriiMotov! Indeed, this seems to be working, at least in recent versions of FastAPI, currently at 0.124.0.

I added this as a test here: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14477

I'll now close this issue. ☕

issues.title: 🚀 Roadmap
issues.body:
## Description

This is a tentative roadmap, I will update it as things evolve. Some things might be discarded, others might be added later. I didn't want to make it fully public as it could raise expectations, but it seems it would be more beneficial for the community to know all the ideas and objectives.

Work on this is alternated (and sometimes mixed) with work on Typer, SQLModel, Asyncer, and others.

Answering questions, issues, and PRs is also intermixed with this, but as in some cases one of these features would solve several issues or questions, or potentially solve something done by one or more PRs, in many cases I focus on this a bit more than on answering specific issues, questions, PRs.

## Other Tools

FastAPI gets a lot of help from the underlying tools and the people behind them, Pydantic, Starlette, Uvicorn. A lot of the work done there reflects in FastAPI, so they are helping FastAPI, in some way helping maintaining it too, because it's all connected.

## Maintenance

The word "maintenance" or "maintainer" doesn't have a very strict definition, and it probably varies a lot from perspective.

A lot of the work related to maintaining FastAPI is done by contributors, you can see the current team here: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/fastapi-people/#team

And you can learn about how the project is managed here: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/management/

I also receive a lot of help from others like the FastAPI Experts: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/fastapi-people/#fastapi-experts

A lot of the maintenance work is done by this amazing community.

If I suddenly died being hit by a bus, there are already mechanisms in place for code inheritance, there are others with GitHub rights to take over, etc. but I have requested to still review each PR myself.

You can help me ensure each existing PR is in shape (has tests, solves the problem, etc.). Make sure you filter out the translation PRs (most of them) unless you speak the language and can review them.

## Secur

comments.body:
This is lovely! 

A suggestion...will it be possible to have a list of PRs mapped with these roadmap items, so that we can check and contribute to those which are not started.
Don't you plan on adding RefreshUrl to the OAuth2 Password Flow?
Is there any plans to add class based view natively support by fastapi?
Small comment regarding `Errors based on RFC 7807`: it has already been obsoleted by RFC 9457 - https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9457.html.

A note on the maintenance section.
> I have requested to still review each PR myself.

That is a major issue IMO. We've deployed a FastAPI app in production for one of our microservices, as an exploratory project to switch over from Flask. We started hitting issues like https://github.com/MagicStack/asyncpg/issues/978, which https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5707 is attempting to fix (see also related issue https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/8424).

I consider that issue be a major one, because it really touches on what makes async programming in Python hard and a little risky without a good framework on top – the errors you get are somewhat probabilistic, the stracktraces cryptic and the issues hard to debug. That PR is almost a year old, and there has been no activity of any kind on it from you, and that worries me. Because of that, my current conclusion from this exploratory project is that FastAPI isn't something I want to rely on in a production environment yet.

Your project is awesome and I love it, like many others. I have a lot of respect for your work here. But with 64k stars it's far beyond a "one-man shop"  OSS project, and having your review every PR isn't realistic anymore. Maybe you could start with a 2nd core reviewer, and see how that works?

issues.title: "Behind a Proxy" changed behaviour in the new version 0.109.0 (in Uvicorn)
issues.body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/10948

This is related to a change in Starlette and Uvicorn, making them more formally follow the ASGI spec, Starlette already does since version 0.35.0 (recently), Uvicorn needs an equivalent change, in process on: https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/pull/2213

More details in the Starlette PR: https://github.com/encode/starlette/pull/2400

<div type='discussions-op-text'>

<sup>Originally posted by **neugeeug** January 12, 2024</sup>
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title here.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
uvicorn rest.main:server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080 --reload --root-path /proxy

server = FastAPI()

# separate spec at /v1/docs
server.mount("/v1", app_v1)
# separate spec at /v2/docs
server.mount("/v2", app_v2)

#where app_v1, app_v2 is like below
app_v1 = FastAPI(openapi_tags=tags_metadata, title="Pet store")
```


### Description

When I run the uvicorn with the `--root-path` option for the above setup, the server is expecting that I include the defined root path in every request e.g:
 http://localhost:8080/v2/docs is not working (404)
 http://localhost:8080/proxy/v2/docs is working

IN version 0.108.0 It

issues.title: Update docs include syntax for source examples
issues.body:
### Privileged issue

- [X] I'm @tiangolo or he asked me directly to create an issue here.

### Issue Content

This is a good first contribution. :nerd_face: 

The code examples shown in the docs are actual Python files. They are even tested in CI, that's why you can always copy paste an example and it will always work, the example is tested.

The way those examples are included in the docs used a specific format. But now there's a new format available that is much simpler and easier to use than the previous one, in particular in complex cases, for example when there are examples in multiple versions of Python.

But not all the docs have the new format yet. The docs should use the new format to include examples. That is the task. :nerd_face: 

**It should be done as one PR per page updated.**

## Simple Example

Before, the format was like:

````markdown
```Python hl_lines="3"
{!../../docs_src/first_steps/tutorial001.py!}
```
````

Now the new format looks like:

````markdown
{* ../../docs_src/first_steps/tutorial001.py hl[3] *}
````

* Instead of `{!` and `!}` it uses `{*` and `*}`
* It no longer has a line above with:

````markdown
```Python
````

* And it no longer has a line below with:

````markdown
```
````

* The highlight is no longer a line with e.g. `hl_lines="3"` (to highlight line 3), but instead in the same line there's a `hl[3]`.

An example PR: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12552

## Multiple Python Versions

There are some cases where there are variants of the same example for multiple versions of Python, or for using `Annotated` or not.

In those cases, the current include examples have syntax for tabs, and notes saying `Annotated` should be preferred. For example:

````markdown
//// tab | Python 3.9+

```Python hl_lines="4  8  12"
{!> ../../docs_src/security/tutorial006_an_py39.py!}
```

////

//// tab | Python 3.8+

```Python hl_lines="2  7  11"
{!> ../../docs_src/securit

First file `fr/docs/advanced/additional-responses.md` I want to update, contains Python directives : `Python hl_lines="3"` but also Json directives like that : `JSON hl_lines="4-16"`
